'FOUR BASIC TEMPERAMENTS' = pre-set categories (choleric, sanguine, melancholic, phlegmatic)
= these 4 types are fixed by nature (ancient Greek theory)
'balance and dominance WITHIN ONE OF THESE'
= personality = which temperament dominates
= this is determined independently of any particular situation
= you ARE choleric/sanguine/etc. in ALL situations
= situation-INDEPENDENT = maximum contrast with situationally-variable iv ✓

[Why ii is less contrasting]
ii: 'hereditary endows... Environment provides impressions'
= heredity = fixed (internal)
= BUT environment ALSO plays a role
= ii partially acknowledges situational/environmental influence
= not as purely fixed as i's four temperaments

[The dichotomy]
Fixed/static (not situational) ↔ Variable/situational
i = maximally fixed (four temperaments) = maximum contrast
ii = partially fixed, partially variable = less contrast
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q22): Modified iv = personality varies with situation. Find what is MOST situation-independent. i = four fixed temperaments = predetermined, applies in ALL situations = maximum contrast. ii = heredity+environment = partly situational = less contrast. Purely fixed categories = maximum contrast with situationally variable.

quiz.question:
[Unit 116 — Meta Pattern]

Q18: chose D (iv) — iv = structural integration / iii = dominant overriding trait pattern
Q20: chose A (ii) — ii includes 'hereditary' (internal) / vi = purely external (mores, social matrix)
Q21: chose C (iv) — iv = integration of internal selves (still internal) / vi = purely external = not related
Q22: chose B (ii) — ii = partially variable / i = four fixed temperaments = maximally situation-independent

Two dichotomies explain all four errors. Which correctly identifies them?
options: All four are vocabulary errors — unfamiliar words

Dichotomy 1 = INTERNAL vs EXTERNAL (Q18, Q20, Q21): trait theory = internal overriding pattern (iii) / situation theory = purely external (vi, not ii which includes hereditary) / factor analysis = NOT vi because vi is external. Dichotomy 2 = FIXED vs VARIABLE/SITUATIONAL (Q18, Q22): trait/temperament theories = fixed across situations (iii, i) / situation theories = variable by context (iv+situations). Classification tool: for each definition, ask (1) internal or external? (2) fixed or situationally variable?
All four are targeting errors — wrong paragraph
All four are paraphrasing errors only
quiz.answer: ② Dichotomy 1 = internal vs external / Dichotomy 2 = fixed vs situationally variable — two questions classify all six definitions
quiz.explanation:
[Two-question classification tool]
Q1: Is the cause of personality INTERNAL (hereditary, traits, selves) or EXTERNAL (mores, customs, social matrix)?
Q2: Is personality FIXED (consistent across situations) or VARIABLE (changes with situation)?

[Classifying all six definitions]
i: internal + FIXED (four temperaments = same always) → Type theory, maximum contrast with situational
ii: internal (hereditary) + partially external (environment) → Mixed
iii: internal + FIXED (extrovert/introvert = consistent trait) → TRAIT THEORY ✓ (Q18)
iv: internal (multiple selves) + can be variable → Integrative
v: internal (affective personality = hard shell) + fixed
vi: EXTERNAL (mores, customs, social matrix) + determines responses → SITUATION THEORY ✓ (Q20)

[Application to each question]
Q18 (trait = overriding/fixed internal): iii = internal + fixed = trait theory ✓
Q20 (situation = external): vi = purely external / ii = mixed internal+external ✗
Q21 (NOT factor analysis = NOT internal building blocks): vi = external = NOT internal = least related ✓
Q22 (contrast with situationally variable): i = most fixed = maximum contrast ✓

[The checklist]
For each theory definition:
① Internal or external?
② Fixed or situationally variable?
= Two checks classify any personality definition
quiz.action:
Meta Rule (Unit 116):
Two classification questions: (1) internal or external? (2) fixed or situationally variable?
Trait theory = internal + fixed. Situation theory = external + variable. Factor analysis = internal (building blocks). Type theory = fixed categories. Apply both questions before choosing.

quiz.question: [Q60 — Logic: How many reviews are 'culinary ciphers'?] Definition: 'culinary ciphers = innocuous-sounding words and phrases designed to OBSCURE rather than enlighten' = must sound HARMLESS on the surface while hiding critical intent Review A: 'adapted for use in germ warfare' Review B: 'horrendous pizza... grudgingly' Review C: 'appalling... absolute disgrace' Review D: 'sponge-dry... bog-papery... biffed' Q: How many of the four criticisms could be classified as culinary ciphers? Your pick — C: 2 Correct — A: 0 Why does every single review FAIL the 'innocuous-sounding' test?
options: C is correct because Review A (germ warfare) and Review D (technical-sounding) use indirect language

ALL four reviews fail because NONE use innocuous-sounding language: A = 'germ warfare' (openly aggressive, not innocuous). B = 'horrendous', 'grudgingly' (bluntly negative). C = 'appalling', 'absolute disgrace' (explicitly harsh). D = 'bog-papery', 'biffed' (vividly negative slang). The cipher test requires language that SOUNDS harmless while hiding criticism — none of these sound harmless. They all openly display their hostility. Answer = 0 = A.
A is wrong because Review D uses technical food terms that could seem neutral
C is correct because Reviews A and D use metaphorical language that obscures the criticism
quiz.answer: ② ALL four reviews openly display hostility — none sound innocuous. 0 qualify = A
quiz.explanation:
[The two-part cipher test]
Condition 1: INNOCUOUS-SOUNDING = words that seem harmless/neutral on the surface
Condition 2: DESIGNED TO OBSCURE = criticism is hidden, not openly stated
BOTH must apply simultaneously

[Testing each review]
Review A: 'adapted for use in GERM WARFARE'
= germ warfare = aggressive military weapon reference
= NOT innocuous-sounding at all
= openly hostile metaphor ✗

Review B: 'HORRENDOUS pizza... GRUDGINGLY'
= horrendous = explicitly terrible
= grudgingly = resentfully (openly negative attitude)
= no hiding ✗

Review C: 'APPALLING... ABSOLUTE DISGRACE'
= appalling = explicitly shocking
= absolute disgrace = maximum condemnation
= no cipher, pure direct attack ✗

Review D: 'SPONGE-DRY... BOG-PAPERY... BIFFED'
= sponge-dry, bog-papery = vivid negative textures
= biffed = British slang for ruined/messed up
= these are VIVIDLY negative, not innocuous
= a reader immediately understands the criticism ✗

[Why C was tempting]
Review A: 'germ warfare' = a METAPHOR → might seem like indirect/coded language
Review D: technical food vocabulary → might seem neutral
But: the cipher definition requires the language to SOUND harmless
= germ warfare sounds hostile, not harmless
= bog-papery sounds unpleasant, not harmless
= metaphorical ≠ innocuous-sounding

[The key distinction]
Metaphorical/indirect ≠ innocuous
Innocuous = sounds genuinely neutral or positive
= none of these reviews sound neutral or positive at any point
= answer = 0 = A
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q60): 'Innocuous-sounding' = must SOUND harmless/neutral on the surface. Test: would a naive reader think this is a compliment or neutral observation? All four reviews sound immediately negative — none pass the innocuous test. Metaphorical ≠ innocuous. 0 qualify = A.

quiz.question:
[Q54 — Targeting: Which shows ways to save money OUTSIDE THE HOME?]

Option A (Joan Bakewell): 'I never waste food... cook a meal... use leftovers... rubber bands and paper clips... Jiffy bag'
Option B (Liberty Ross): 'avoid expensive taxis... walking everywhere... drive my Skoda, fuel efficient'
Option C (Jonathan Aitken): 'visit a supermarket at 11 pm... products past their sell-by date'

Q: Which of the options shows ways to save money OUTSIDE THE HOME?

Your pick — C
Correct — B

Why does 'supermarket' (C) fail the 'outside the home' test?
options: C is correct because going to a supermarket is an activity that happens outside the home

The test is: where does the SAVING BEHAVIOUR occur? C's supermarket trip = outside the home, BUT the saving is achieved by buying food THAT WILL BE CONSUMED AT HOME. The destination of the saving = home. B: 'avoid expensive taxis... walking everywhere... fuel efficient car' = the saving itself occurs in TRANSIT/OUTSIDE contexts — transportation costs are inherently outside-the-home expenses. B's savings stay outside.
B is wrong because driving a car involves coming home eventually
C is correct because supermarket visits are the most common outside-home savings activity
quiz.answer: ② C = supermarket (outside location, but savings serve HOME consumption) / B = transportation costs (inherently outside-the-home domain) = the saving itself is external
quiz.explanation:
[The spatial test: where does the saving APPLY?]

Option C: 'visit supermarket at 11 pm, buy half-price food'
= The ACT occurs outside (supermarket)
= But the SAVING applies to food that goes HOME
= the purpose/destination = home consumption
= saving = home-linked ✗

Option B: 'avoid taxis, walk, drive fuel-efficient car'
= taxis, congestion charges = transportation costs
= these costs exist ONLY outside the home
= walking = outside activity
= fuel efficiency = outside mobility cost
= the saving itself = entirely in the outside-home domain ✓

[Why A also fails]
A: 'never waste food, use leftovers, collect rubber bands'
= all household management activities
= rubber bands, Jiffy bags = home/office supply domain
= entirely within-home savings ✗

[The key distinction]
C: outside location → saves money → for home use
B: outside domain (transport) → saves money → on outside costs
= B's savings have no home component
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q54): 'Outside the home' = the SAVING BEHAVIOUR must apply to external costs. C = buys food outside but for home consumption = home-linked. B = transportation costs (taxis, fuel) = inherently outside domain. Ask: 'What is the saving being applied TO?' Transport costs = outside. Food for home = home.

quiz.question:
[Q55 — Paraphrase: Who is most PROUD of their STINGY ways?]

Two conditions: PROUD (boasting, self-satisfied) + STINGY (miserly, extreme frugality)

Option A (Joan Bakewell): 'I collect rubber bands and paper clips... I haven't bought a Jiffy bag in TEN YEARS.'
Option D (Keith Harris): 'I was earning £10 a week... I put my wages straight into the bank. I was very careful.'

Q: Who is most proud of their stingy ways?

Your pick — D
Correct — A

Why does 'ten years without buying a Jiffy bag' = proud + stingy while D = careful but not stingy?
options: D is correct because putting wages straight into the bank is a very disciplined form of frugality

STINGY requires: extreme, almost irrational frugality about trivial things. D: earning £10 and banking it = CAREFUL and DILIGENT — a sensible financial habit, not extreme miserliness. A: collecting rubber bands and paper clips + 'haven't bought a Jiffy bag in TEN YEARS' = extreme penny-pinching about trivial items (Jiffy bags are cheap) = genuinely stingy. A also delivers this with pride: 'I never waste food... I also collect...' = declarative pride. D sounds modest/careful, not proudly stingy.
A is wrong because rubber bands and paper clips are just recycling habits
D is correct because strict budgeting from a young age is the most impressive form of frugality
quiz.answer: ② D = careful/diligent (sensible, not extreme) / A = collecting trivial items + 10 years no Jiffy bag = extreme miserliness delivered with declarative pride = stingy + proud
quiz.explanation:
[Two-condition test]
Condition 1: STINGY = miserly, extreme about trivial things
Condition 2: PROUD = boastful, self-satisfied tone

[Testing D]
'Earning £10 a week... put wages straight into bank... very careful'
= banking wages = prudent financial habit
= 'very careful' = measured, responsible
= NOT extreme or irrational = NOT stingy
= tone = reflective, not boastful
= fails both conditions ✗

[Testing A]
'I collect RUBBER BANDS and PAPER CLIPS'
= collecting cheap office supplies others throw away = miserly
'I haven't bought a JIFFY BAG in TEN YEARS'
= Jiffy bags = very cheap postal bags = refusing to buy them for a DECADE = extreme
= this is genuinely stingy (irrational about trivial, cheap items)
= delivered as a boast ('I haven't...') = PROUD ✓

[The tone of pride in A]
'I NEVER waste food' = declarative
'I ALSO collect rubber bands' = adding another proud achievement
'haven't bought a Jiffy bag in TEN YEARS' = the duration itself is the boast
= A presents extreme frugality as a BADGE OF HONOUR = proud stingy ✓

[Why C also fails]
C: 'living on a wage of £5.60... in prison... bankrupt'
= survival necessity, not pride
= forced frugality, not chosen with pride
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q55): 'Proud of stingy ways' = BOTH conditions: (1) extreme/irrational frugality about trivial things AND (2) boastful/proud tone. A = rubber bands + paper clips + 10 years no Jiffy bag = stingy about trivial items, delivered as achievements. D = careful banking = sensible, not stingy. C = survival necessity = not proud.

quiz.question:
[Q56 — Logic: Which quote is most OUT OF SYNC with the other three?]

A: 'never waste food... use leftovers... collect rubber bands... recycle'
B: 'avoid taxis... walk everywhere... fuel efficient car'
C: 'supermarket at 11 pm... half-price products'
D: 'earning £10... put wages straight into the bank... very careful with money'

Q: Which has a tone most out of sync with the other three?

Your pick — A
Correct — D

What category do A, B, C share that D does not?
options: A is wrong because it is the most extreme example, not the most different in type

A, B, C all describe specific CONSUMPTION BEHAVIOUR CHANGES (how you spend differently day-to-day): A = food habits + recycling, B = transport choices, C = shopping timing. All three = active lifestyle frugality tips with specific behaviours. D: 'put wages into the bank' = a FINANCIAL STORAGE action, not a consumption behaviour change. D describes what you do WITH money after earning, not HOW you spend differently. Different category = out of sync = D.
D is correct because banking was a common and obvious savings strategy
A is most out of sync because it covers too many different areas (food, supplies, etc.)
quiz.answer: ② A+B+C = consumption behaviour changes (specific frugal actions in daily life) / D = financial storage action (banking wages) = different category = out of sync
quiz.explanation:
[Category analysis]
The question: tone 'out of sync' = different TYPE of saving advice

[A, B, C — all share the same category]
A: 'never WASTE food... use LEFTOVERS... COLLECT rubber bands'
= active behaviour changes in daily consumption
= HOW you use things differently

B: 'AVOID expensive taxis... WALK everywhere... DRIVE fuel efficient car'
= active behaviour changes in transportation consumption
= HOW you travel differently

C: 'VISIT supermarket at 11 pm... BUY half-price products'
= active behaviour change in shopping consumption
= WHEN and HOW you buy differently

Common theme A+B+C: SPECIFIC ACTIONS that change consumption behaviour

[D — different category]
'I was EARNING £10... I put wages STRAIGHT INTO THE BANK'
= what you do with income AFTER earning
= financial management, not consumption behaviour
= no specific frugal action in daily life
= no 'instead of X, do Y' structure
= purely about SAVING vs SPENDING the money you have

[D is out of sync]
A, B, C = consumption frugality tips (how to spend less day-to-day)
D = income management tip (save what you earn)
= different advice category = out of sync ✓
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q56): 'Out of sync' = find the one that belongs to a DIFFERENT CATEGORY. A+B+C = specific consumption behaviour changes (frugal daily actions). D = banking wages = financial storage, not consumption behaviour. Category difference: daily frugality actions vs income management. D = out of sync.

source: Unit 113 — Meta: Spatial Test + Tone + Category

quiz.question:
[Unit 113 — Meta Pattern]

Q54: chose C (supermarket) — outside location but saving serves home consumption
Q55: chose D (banking) — careful/diligent but not stingy or proudly boastful
Q56: chose A (food recycling) — extreme but same category as B+C

Three tools cover all three errors. Which correctly identifies them?
options: All three are vocabulary errors

Tool 1 = SPATIAL DESTINATION TEST (Q54): 'outside the home' = saving must apply to EXTERNAL costs. C = buys food for home use = home-linked. B = transport costs = entirely external. Tool 2 = DUAL CONDITION TEST (Q55): both PROUD + STINGY must apply. D = careful/sensible (not stingy). A = trivial item hoarding (stingy) + boastful declarations (proud) = both ✓. Tool 3 = CATEGORY CLASSIFICATION (Q56): A+B+C = consumption behaviour changes / D = income storage = different category = out of sync.
All three are targeting errors
All three are paraphrasing errors only
quiz.answer: ② Spatial destination + dual condition + category classification — three tools
quiz.explanation:
[Tool 1: Spatial destination test (Q54)]
Question: 'outside the home'
= not where the ACTION occurs, but where the SAVING APPLIES
C: action outside → saving for home use = home-linked ✗
B: action outside → saving on external costs (transport) = truly outside ✓

[Tool 2: Dual condition test (Q55)]
Proud AND stingy — both must apply
D: careful/sensible → neither stingy (not extreme) nor proudly boastful ✗
A: rubber bands/Jiffy bags → stingy (trivial extreme) + declarative boasting → proud ✓

[Tool 3: Category classification (Q56)]
Define the dominant category of A, B, C:
= specific consumption behaviour changes
D: income storage (banking) = different category
= out of sync = D ✓
A = extreme version of same category = not out of sync ✗
quiz.action:
Meta Rule (Unit 113):
Spatial: 'outside home' = where the saving APPLIES, not where the action occurs.
Dual condition: test BOTH required qualities separately — one failure = eliminated.
Category: 'out of sync' = find the one that belongs to a different TYPE of advice, not just an extreme version.

quiz.question:
[Q58 — Targeting: Classify 'In the Children's Hospital (1935)']

Poem evidence:
'Now let the legless boy show the great lady / How well he can manage his crutches'
= a royal/noble visitor watching a disabled boy perform for her approval

'But would the sound of your sticks on the floor / Thundered in her skull for evermore!'
= final lines: fierce anger — the poet wishes the sound would haunt the visitor

Poetry types: A=descriptive/image/mood, B=advice/philosophy, C=personal experience, D=criticism through satire

Q: This poem belongs to type ______.

Your pick — A (descriptive/image)
Correct — D (criticism through satire)

Why does the final couplet eliminate all types except D?
options: A is correct because the poem vividly describes the hospital ward and the boy's crutches

The final two lines: 'But would the sound of your sticks... Thundered in her skull for evermore!' = explicit wish for the visitor to be haunted by guilt = ANGER directed at the royal visitor's superficial pity. This is SATIRICAL CRITICISM: the 'great lady' is mocked for treating the boy's suffering as performance. A (descriptive) = no criticism. B (philosophy) = no advice given. C (personal experience) = not the poet's own suffering. D = criticism of the lady's callousness through bitter irony = D ✓
A is correct because the poem creates a vivid image of the hospital setting
C is correct because the poet witnesses the scene personally
quiz.answer: ② Final couplet = anger/wish for guilt = satirical attack on the visitor's shallowness = D / A = no critical voice, just imagery
quiz.explanation:
[The targeting failure: 'legless, crutches' → philosophical interpretation]
The poem DOES contain vivid imagery (crutches, ward, sticks)
= this explains why A (descriptive) seems plausible
= BUT imagery alone ≠ the poem's PURPOSE

[The satirical target: the 'great lady']
'Now let the legless boy SHOW the great lady'
= the boy is made to perform for her
= 'How WELL he can manage his crutches'
= ironic: she evaluates his suffering as a demonstration

'The will of the Princess. Come, Tommy'
= the child is commanded for royal entertainment
= the poet's tone here = sickened, angry

[The clinching final lines]
'But would the sound of your sticks on the floor / Thundered in her skull for evermore!'
= 'your sticks' = addressed to the lady
= the poet WISHES she would be haunted
= explicit expression of anger at her callousness
= SATIRE = using the superficial visit to expose the lady's moral emptiness

[Why the subjective reading (A) is the error]
You focused on: legless + crutches = hospital imagery
You assigned: philosophical/respectful meaning to 'the loss'
But the tone: anger, irony, haunting = CRITICAL, not respectful
= you imported your own emotional response to disability
= the poem's actual target = the royal visitor's shallowness
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q58): In poetry classification — scan ALL stanzas before classifying. The FINAL lines usually reveal the true purpose. 'Thundered in her skull' = anger at the visitor = satire (D). Early vivid imagery does not make a poem 'descriptive' if the dominant purpose is criticism.

quiz.question:
[Q61 — Classify 'from The Vanity of Human Wishes']

Poem evidence:
'Where then shall hope and fear their objects find?'
'Must dull suspense corrupt the stagnant mind?'
→ questions about human limitation and mental stagnation

Final lines:
'But leave to heaven the measure and the choice,
Safe in his power, whose eyes discern afar
... Secure whate'er he gives, he gives the best.'
→ resolution: submit to divine providence, accept what God gives

Poetry types: A=descriptive, B=advice/philosophy, C=personal experience, D=criticism through satire

Q: This poem belongs to type ______.

Your pick — D (satire) — 'negative words like suspense, stagnant, ambush'
Correct — B (advice/philosophy)

How do the FINAL lines reveal B and eliminate D?
options: D is correct because 'suspense', 'stagnant', 'specious', 'ambush' create a critical, mocking tone

Satire (D) requires: mocking a specific target with irony. The poem does use dark vocabulary BUT the final resolution is 'leave to heaven... he gives the best' = ACCEPTANCE and SUBMISSION to divine providence. This is not satire (mocking) but PHILOSOPHY (advice about how to live: accept God's will, do not rely on earthly hope/fear). The dark words describe the human condition being examined, not a satirical target being mocked.
B is wrong because Samuel Johnson is criticising human vanity, not giving neutral advice
D is correct because 'The Vanity of Human Wishes' in the title signals satirical criticism
quiz.answer: ② Final lines = 'leave to heaven... he gives the best' = acceptance/philosophical resolution / satire requires mockery of a target — this poem resolves with wisdom, not mockery
quiz.explanation:
[The paraphrase failure: dark words → satire assumption]
You saw: suspense, stagnant, ambush, specious (all negative)
You concluded: negative tone = satire/criticism
But: negative vocabulary ≠ satirical tone

[What satire requires]
Satire (D) = mocking a specific target through irony/exaggeration
= the poet LAUGHS AT or SCORNS something specific
= example from Unit 112: 'The English Are So Nice!' = mocks English niceness

[What this poem actually does]
'Where then shall hope and fear their objects find?'
= examining human existential questions
'Must dull suspense corrupt the stagnant mind?'
= describing the danger of passive/stagnant living

[The CRUCIAL final lines — the resolution]
'But leave to heaven the measure and the choice'
= advice: surrender judgment to God
'Safe in his power, whose eyes discern afar'
= trust in God's larger vision
'Secure whate'er he gives, he gives the best'
= accept divine providence: whatever God gives is best

= this is PHILOSOPHICAL ADVICE about how to live
= B: poetry that offers advice or philosophy ✓

[The title is not satire in content]
'Vanity of Human Wishes' = a philosophical observation
= human hopes are vain/futile (philosophical)
= not a mocking satire of specific people
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q61): Don't classify by vocabulary — classify by PURPOSE and RESOLUTION. Dark vocabulary = examining a difficult theme (philosophical). Final lines reveal purpose: 'leave to heaven... he gives the best' = accept divine will = PHILOSOPHICAL ADVICE (B). Satire needs a mocked target; this poem ends with acceptance, not mockery.

source: Unit 112 — Meta: Final Lines + Purpose vs Vocabulary

quiz.question:
[Unit 112 — Meta Pattern]

Q58: chose A (descriptive) — focused on imagery/vocabulary, missed the satirical anger in final lines
Q61: chose D (satire) — saw negative words, guessed satire, missed the philosophical resolution in final lines

Both errors share the same root. Which correctly identifies it?
options: Both are vocabulary errors — unfamiliar poetic words

Both errors = classified by SURFACE VOCABULARY instead of FINAL PURPOSE. Q58: vivid imagery (crutches, ward) → assumed descriptive, but final lines = satirical anger at the lady. Q61: dark words (suspense, stagnant, ambush) → assumed satire, but final lines = philosophical acceptance. Fix: always read to the END. Final lines = where the poet reveals their TRUE purpose. Ask: 'What does the poem resolve to? What is the poet ultimately doing?'
Both are targeting errors — wrong stanza analysed
Both are paraphrasing errors about individual words
quiz.answer: ② Both classified by early/surface vocabulary instead of reading the final resolution — final lines reveal true purpose
quiz.explanation:
[The shared error]
Q58: early stanzas = vivid hospital imagery → classified as A (descriptive)
FINAL LINES: 'Thundered in her skull for evermore!' = satire/anger = D ✓

Q61: early stanzas = dark vocabulary (suspense, stagnant) → classified as D (satire)
FINAL LINES: 'he gives the best' = philosophical acceptance = B ✓

[The fix: final lines = purpose declaration]
Poets typically BUILD toward their point
= early stanzas: set up the subject/problem
= final stanzas: deliver the POINT/RESOLUTION

For classification questions:
1. Read the final lines FIRST (or last)
2. Ask: 'What is the poet ULTIMATELY doing?'
3. Then classify:
   = anger/mockery at end → D (satire)
   = advice/acceptance at end → B (philosophy)
   = celebration/mood at end → A (descriptive)
   = personal reflection at end → C (personal experience)

[Vocabulary ≠ classification]
Negative vocabulary → can appear in ANY type
Philosophical poems use negative vocabulary to describe the problem
Satirical poems use ironic/positive vocabulary to mock
Descriptive poems use vivid vocabulary to paint pictures
= vocabulary alone does not determine type
quiz.action: Meta Rule (Unit 112): For poetry classification — always read to the END. Final lines = the poet's true purpose revealed. Q58 final = anger at visitor = satire. Q61 final = accept divine will = philosophy. Never classify from early-stanza vocabulary alone.

quiz.question: [Q28 — Paraphrase: Which quality encourages STAFF DEVELOPMENT in accord with B6?] B6 = 'The attitude of a staff member's immediate BOSS to staff development' L6: 'Trusts people; delegates WHOLE JOBS; keeps promises; PROMOTES FROM WITHIN' A6: 'Manages; parcels out jobs; oversees all major developments; uses JOB-HIRE to bring OUTSIDE EXPERIENCE' Q: Which leadership quality most likely encourages staff development? Your pick — D: A6 Correct — B: L6 Why does 'promotes from within' = staff development and 'outside job-hire' = its opposite?
options: D is correct because A6 brings in experienced people who can mentor existing staff

Staff development = growing existing employees' skills and advancing them. L6: 'promotes FROM WITHIN' = existing staff are developed and advanced internally. 'Delegates WHOLE JOBS' = gives full responsibility = develops capability. A6: 'uses job-hire to bring OUTSIDE EXPERIENCE' = bypasses existing staff by hiring externally = the opposite of developing internal staff. A6 replaces staff with outsiders; L6 grows staff from within.
B is wrong because delegates whole jobs could overwhelm under-developed staff
D is correct because A6 'oversees all major developments' shows investment in growth
quiz.answer: ② L6 = promotes from within + delegates whole jobs = internal development / A6 = outside job-hire = bypasses and replaces internal staff = opposite of development
quiz.explanation:
[Paraphrase chain: staff development → L6]
Staff development = growing and advancing existing employees

L6: 'PROMOTES FROM WITHIN'
= existing staff rise through the ranks
= their development is rewarded with advancement ✓

L6: 'DELEGATES WHOLE JOBS'
= gives full responsibility to staff
= they learn by doing complete tasks
= builds capability and trust = development ✓

[Why A6 is the OPPOSITE]
A6: 'uses JOB-HIRE to bring OUTSIDE EXPERIENCE into the company'
= when expertise is needed, they hire externally
= existing staff are NOT developed for those roles
= internal staff are bypassed in favour of ready-made external hires
= A6 = anti-development by replacement ✗

[The key distinction]
Development = internal growth path (L6)
Replacement = external acquisition (A6)
= these are fundamentally opposite approaches to building capability
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q28): 'Staff development' = growing INTERNAL employees. L6 'promotes from within' + 'delegates whole jobs' = direct match. A6 'outside job-hire' = replaces rather than develops internal staff = opposite. Development = internal growth path.

quiz.question: [Q29 — Paraphrase: Which leadership quality FORESHADOWS B7?] B7 = 'The MODES OF PARTICIPATION of management in staff development' = how management actively PARTICIPATES — specific modes/methods of involvement L2: 'Available, good listener' L8: 'MBWA (manages by walking around)' Q: Which leadership practice most likely foreshadows a positive interpretation of B7? Your pick — B: L2 Correct — D: L8 Why is 'walking around' a MODE of participation while 'listening' is not?
options: B is correct because being available and a good listener enables participation through open communication

B7 requires: specific MODES (methods/styles) of PARTICIPATION (active involvement). L2 'available, good listener' = passive availability — people come to you. L8 'MBWA — manages by walking around' = the manager GOES TO the staff = active, visible MODE of participation. MBWA is a recognised management methodology that directly describes HOW management participates in staff development. Listening = passive reception; MBWA = active participatory mode.
D is wrong because walking around is informal and doesn't constitute a formal mode
B is correct because communication is the foundation of all participation modes
quiz.answer: ② L2 = passive availability (people come to you) / L8 = MBWA = active mode of going to staff = specific participatory METHOD = direct match for B7
quiz.explanation:
[B7 decoded]
'Modes of PARTICIPATION of MANAGEMENT in staff development'
= how management actively ENGAGES with staff
= specific METHODS of being involved
= requires: active, visible, directional behaviour

[L2 → passive reception]
'Available, good LISTENER'
= staff can approach the leader
= the leader receives input
= PASSIVE: waiting for participation to come to them
= not a MODE of active management participation ✗

[L8 → active participatory mode]
'MBWA — manages by WALKING AROUND'
= the manager physically moves through the workplace
= engages with staff in THEIR environment
= proactive, visible, directional
= a specific named management METHODOLOGY
= directly = a MODE of management participation ✓

[The key distinction]
Passive reception (L2) ≠ active mode of participation
MBWA = named methodology = specific mode
= L8 = direct paraphrase of 'modes of participation' ✓
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q29): 'Modes of participation' = active methods of management involvement. L8 MBWA = specific named methodology for active management participation. L2 = passive availability (people come to leader) ≠ an active mode. Active method vs passive reception.

quiz.question: [Q30 — Paraphrase: Which climate aspect is most aligned to quality A4?] A4: 'Establishes, and WORKS WITHIN, POLICY' = creates and consistently adheres to policy rules B5: 'The company's accepted balance of INTEGRITY, RELIABILITY and innovation' B8: 'The TIME-FRAME EXPECTATION for results from any particular development session' Q: Which B aspect is most aligned to A4? Your pick — D: B8 Correct — A: B5 How does 'works within policy' = integrity and reliability?
options: D is correct because establishing policy involves setting time-frames for implementation

'Establishes and works WITHIN policy' = creates rules AND consistently follows them. Working within policy = acting consistently according to agreed standards = RELIABILITY (you can be counted on). Establishing policy = defining principles and rules = INTEGRITY (adherence to principles). B5: 'accepted balance of INTEGRITY, RELIABILITY' = direct paraphrase of A4's policy-based management. B8 (time-frame) has no logical connection to policy adherence.
A is wrong because integrity and reliability are personal traits, not organisational policy
D is correct because policy sets time boundaries for decision-making
quiz.answer: ② 'Works within policy' = consistent adherence = RELIABILITY / 'Establishes policy' = principled rule-setting = INTEGRITY / B5 = direct paraphrase / B8 = time-frame has no policy connection
quiz.explanation:
[A4 decoded]
'Establishes policy' = creates organisational rules and principles
'Works WITHIN policy' = consistently operates according to those rules
= does not deviate from established principles

[Paraphrase map → B5]
'Establishes policy' → INTEGRITY
= integrity = principled conduct, adherence to standards
= creating and maintaining policy = acting with integrity

'Works WITHIN policy' → RELIABILITY
= reliability = you can be counted on to behave consistently
= consistently following policy = reliable behaviour

[B5: 'integrity, reliability and innovation']
= A4 maps directly to: integrity (principled) + reliability (consistent)
= B5 ✓

[Why B8 has no connection]
B8 = 'time-frame expectation for results'
= about WHEN outcomes are expected
= policy adherence has nothing to do with time-frames
= different domain entirely ✗

[The function vs value distinction]
A4 = describes a BEHAVIOUR (following policy)
B5 = describes a VALUE (integrity + reliability)
= behaviour → underlying value = correct paraphrase direction
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q30): 'Works within policy' = consistent rule-following = RELIABILITY. 'Establishes policy' = principled standard-setting = INTEGRITY. B5 captures both. B8 (time-frame) has zero logical connection to policy. Match BEHAVIOUR to underlying VALUE.

quiz.question:
[Q31 — Paraphrase: Which grouping encourages PATRONAGE?]

Patronage = personal favours based on relationships, not merit
= requires: personal closeness, accessibility, informal bonds

A: L3 & L5 — 'Decisive and tough' + 'Tolerates open disagreement'
B: L1 & L2 — 'Common touch, gets hands dirty' + 'Available, good listener'

Q: Which grouping most likely encourages patronage by leaders?

Your pick — A
Correct — B

Why do 'common touch' and 'availability' create the conditions for patronage?
options: A is correct because decisive tough leaders create loyal followers who depend on their protection

Patronage = personal favours based on who you know/like, not merit. Conditions for patronage: personal familiarity + private access. L1 'common touch, gets hands dirty' = the leader creates PERSONAL BONDS with staff (informal, friendly). L2 'available, good listener' = staff can always privately access the leader. These two qualities create intimate personal relationships = the exact social foundation for patronage (favouring those you're close to). L3 (decisive/tough) + L5 (tolerates disagreement) = professional conflict-management, not relationship-building = not patronage conditions.
B is wrong because a good listener treats everyone equally
A is correct because decisive leaders choose personal favourites to implement decisions
quiz.answer: ② L1 (personal bond through common touch) + L2 (private access through availability) = social foundation for patronage / L3+L5 = professional conflict traits ≠ relationship-based patronage
quiz.explanation:
[Patronage mechanism]
Patronage = favouring people based on PERSONAL RELATIONSHIP, not merit
= requires: ① personal closeness ② private access ③ informal bond

[L1 → personal closeness]
'Common touch, gets hands DIRTY'
= leader works alongside staff informally
= creates a sense of personal connection and familiarity
= 'he's one of us' feeling = personal bond ✓

[L2 → private access]
'AVAILABLE, good listener'
= staff can always approach the leader directly
= private conversations are possible
= personal requests can be made = patronage channel ✓

[Combined: L1 + L2 = patronage conditions]
Personal closeness (L1) + private access (L2)
= staff develop personal relationships with the leader
= patronage: leader favours those they're close to

[Why L3+L5 don't create patronage]
L3: 'decisive and tough; seeks immediate workable solutions'
= professional task focus, not relationship building
L5: 'tolerates open disagreement'
= open, transparent conflict handling
= the opposite of private/personal favouritism ✗
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q31): Patronage = personal favours need personal closeness + private access. L1 'common touch' = personal bond. L2 'available' = private access channel. These create the social conditions for patronage. L3+L5 = professional conflict traits = no personal bond = no patronage foundation.

quiz.question: [Q32 — Logic: Which B principle is LEAST LIKELY to be adopted by L1-L9 leaders?] Key leader trait: L4 = 'SIMPLIFIES AND REMOVES CONTROLS' B1: 'Development of staff is evident in the changing of their goals, ideals and motivation' B4: 'Defined objectives, planned procedures and OTHER CONTROLS are NECESSARY for aligning development' Q: Which B principle is least likely to be adopted by document A leaders? Your pick — A: B1 Correct — D: B4 Why is B4 (controls necessary) directly contradicted by L4 (removes controls)?
options: A is correct because changing goals and motivation is inconsistent with clear leadership direction

Find the DIRECT CONTRADICTION between L1-L9 and B1-B4. L4: 'SIMPLIFIES AND REMOVES CONTROLS' = the leader's approach explicitly ELIMINATES controls. B4: 'controls ARE NECESSARY for aligning development' = explicitly states controls are essential. These are polar opposites: L4 removes what B4 declares necessary. B1 (changing goals/motivation) has no direct contradiction in the leader traits — it could co-exist. B4 vs L4 = perfect antithesis.
D is wrong because some leaders might still use controls selectively
A is correct because motivation-focused development seems too soft for decisive leaders
quiz.answer: ② L4 = 'removes controls' DIRECTLY contradicts B4 = 'controls are necessary' — perfect antithesis / B1 = no direct contradiction in leader traits
quiz.explanation:
[The direct contradiction test]
For 'least likely to be adopted' = find the B principle that DIRECTLY CONTRADICTS a core leader trait

[L4 vs B4 — the perfect antithesis]
L4: 'Simplifies and REMOVES CONTROLS'
= the leader's explicit policy = eliminate controls

B4: 'Defined objectives, planned procedures and other CONTROLS ARE NECESSARY'
= the explicit position = controls are essential/required

= REMOVES ↔ NECESSARY
= polar opposition on the same topic (controls)
= a leader characterised by L4 would fundamentally reject B4 ✓

[Why B1 is not the least likely]
B1: 'Development is evident in the changing of goals, ideals and motivation'
= about how to identify development
= no L1-L9 trait explicitly opposes this
= some leaders might disagree, but no DIRECT contradiction
= B1 ≠ least likely ✗

[The logical structure]
Least likely = most directly contradicted
Direct contradiction = same topic, opposite position
Controls: L4 (removes) ↔ B4 (necessary) = same topic, polar opposite positions
= B4 = least likely to be adopted by leaders who embody L4
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q32): 'Least likely' = most directly contradicted. Test each B against all L1-L9 traits. L4 'removes controls' vs B4 'controls necessary' = same topic, polar opposite = direct antithesis = D. B1 has no direct L-trait contradiction.

quiz.question: [Q34 — Paraphrase: Which B principles relate to HABITUAL achievement?] Habitual = repeated over time, consistent, ongoing = requires: ACTION (repeated behaviour) + TIME (sustained over a period) B3: 'Development requires ACTION by and INVOLVEMENT of the staff member' B8: 'The TIME-FRAME EXPECTATION for results from any particular development session' B4: 'Defined objectives, planned procedures and controls... for aligning development' B6: 'The ATTITUDE of a staff member's immediate boss to staff development' Q: Which pair most relates to habitual achievement? Your pick — B: B4 & B6 Correct — C: B3 & B8 Why does action + time-frame = habitual, while attitude + controls = not habitual?
options: B is correct because B4 (controls) ensures consistency and B6 (boss attitude) drives repeated motivation

'Habitual' = repeated actions over time. B3 'ACTION and INVOLVEMENT' = the repeated behaviour component (what you keep doing). B8 'TIME-FRAME EXPECTATION' = the sustained duration component (over how long). Action × Time = HABIT. B4 (controls) + B6 (boss attitude) = structural conditions, not the mechanism of habit formation itself. Habit = doing something repeatedly (B3) across time (B8).
C is wrong because time-frame is just about scheduling, not habit formation
B is correct because boss attitude determines whether staff keep trying over time
quiz.answer: ② B3 = repeated ACTION component of habit / B8 = TIME component of habit / Action × Time = habitual / B4+B6 = structural conditions, not the habit mechanism
quiz.explanation:
[Habitual decoded]
Habitual = performed repeatedly, consistently, over time
= NOT a one-off event
= requires TWO elements: DOING IT (action) + SUSTAINED OVER TIME

[B3 → action component]
'Development requires ACTION by and INVOLVEMENT of the staff member'
= staff must actively and repeatedly DO things
= involvement = ongoing participation
= the BEHAVIOUR component of habituation ✓

[B8 → time component]
'TIME-FRAME EXPECTATION for results'
= results are expected over a DURATION
= not instant — sustained across time
= the DURATION component of habituation ✓

[B3 + B8 = habitual achievement]
Repeated action (B3) sustained over time (B8)
= the mechanism of habit formation
= C: B3 & B8 ✓

[Why B4+B6 don't capture 'habitual']
B4: 'planned procedures and controls'
= structural conditions/framework
= describes HOW development is organised, not the repetition over time

B6: 'boss attitude'
= an influence factor
= describes a person's stance, not a repeated-action mechanism

= B4+B6 = contextual conditions / B3+B8 = the mechanism itself
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q34): 'Habitual' = action × time. B3 = repeated action (involvement). B8 = time-frame (duration). These two together = the habit mechanism. B4 = controls (structure), B6 = attitude (influence) = contextual factors, not the habit mechanism. Habit = what you do (B3) for how long (B8).

quiz.question:
[Unit 111 — Meta Pattern]

Q28: chose D (A6=outside hire) — outside hire = replaces internal staff ≠ develops them
Q29: chose B (L2=available) — passive reception ≠ active mode of participation
Q30: chose D (B8=time-frame) — no logical connection between policy and time
Q31: chose A (L3+L5=decisive) — professional conflict traits ≠ personal bond for patronage
Q32: chose A (B1) — no direct contradiction / B4 vs L4 = direct antithesis
Q34: chose B (B4+B6) — structure+attitude ≠ the action×time habit mechanism

One shared principle explains most errors. Which correctly identifies it?
options: All six are vocabulary errors — unfamiliar management terms

All six = cross-document paraphrase failure: the question gives a concept (staff development, participation, policy, patronage, habitual) and requires mapping it to the CORRECT L or B item. The error = choosing an item that SOUNDS related but does not match the SPECIFIC MECHANISM or CONCEPT. Fix: define the concept precisely first (what EXACTLY does this word mean?), then test each option against that precise definition.
All six are targeting errors — wrong document consulted
All six are Too Extreme errors
quiz.answer: ② Cross-document paraphrase failure: chose related but imprecise matches. Fix: define the concept precisely before testing options.
quiz.explanation:
[The shared error pattern]
Each question gives a CONCEPT and asks which L or B item matches
The error = choosing something in the same general DOMAIN but wrong MECHANISM

Q28: staff development → chose outside hire (same domain: hiring) but wrong direction (replaces not develops)
Q29: modes of participation → chose good listener (same domain: engagement) but passive not active mode
Q30: policy adherence → chose time-frame (same domain: management) but wrong connection
Q31: patronage → chose decisive/tough (same domain: leadership) but professional not personal
Q32: least likely → chose B1 (same domain: development principles) but no direct contradiction
Q34: habitual → chose controls+attitude (same domain: climate) but structure not mechanism

[The fix: precise definition first]
Before matching, define the concept:
'Staff development' = internal growth and advancement (not replacement)
'Modes of participation' = ACTIVE methods of involvement (not passive reception)
'Policy' = principled rule-following = INTEGRITY + RELIABILITY
'Patronage' = personal relationship favouritism (needs bond + access)
'Least likely' = direct contradiction (same topic, opposite position)
'Habitual' = repeated action (B3) × sustained time (B8)

[Then test each option against the precise definition]
= reject options that are in the right domain but wrong mechanism
quiz.action: Meta Rule (Unit 111): Cross-document paraphrase = define the concept PRECISELY before matching. Generic domain proximity ≠ correct paraphrase. Test each option: does it match the SPECIFIC MECHANISM, not just the general area?

quiz.question: [Q48 — Vocab: Why does AAVSO appear under 'see American Association of Variable Star Observers'?] Instructions: 'Cross-references (using see) direct the reader from acronyms or synonyms or sub-entries or alternate forms of entries to main entries.' Index: 'AAVSO see American Association of Variable Star Observers' Q: The entry under AAVSO refers to AAVSO because AAVSO is ______. Your pick — D: a subentry to a main entry Correct — C: an acronym for another entry What is an acronym and why does AAVSO qualify?
options: D is correct because AAVSO appears under the main entry American Association

Acronym = a word formed from the INITIAL LETTERS of a longer name. AAVSO = A(merican) A(ssociation of) V(ariable) S(tar) O(bservers) = each letter = first letter of a word in the full name = classic acronym. The instructions state 'see' is used for acronyms → C is the direct match. D (subentry) = items listed UNDER a main heading in the index, not a separate abbreviated form.
C is wrong because AAVSO could simply be an alternate form of the name
D is correct because AAVSO is listed separately as a sub-item under the main entry
quiz.answer: ② AAVSO = A+A+V+S+O = initial letters of each word = acronym / instructions: 'see' used for acronyms = C
quiz.explanation:
[Acronym definition]
acronym = a word formed from the FIRST LETTERS of a series of words
A - American
A - Association of
V - Variable
S - Star
O - Observers
= AAVSO = classic acronym ✓

[Instructions → C]
'see directs reader FROM acronyms... TO main entries'
= AAVSO (acronym) → see → American Association (main entry)
= C: 'an acronym for another entry' = direct instruction match ✓

[Why D (subentry) is wrong]
Subentry = an item listed UNDER a main heading (indented below)
= AAVSO appears as its own heading, not indented under another
= it's a separate entry that REFERS elsewhere via 'see'
= not a subentry but a cross-reference from an acronym

[Key vocab to know]
acronym (약어, 두문자어) = word made from initial letters: NATO, NASA, AAVSO
synonym (동의어) = different word, same meaning
subentry = item listed beneath a main heading in the index
cross-reference = 'see' or 'see also' directing to another entry
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q48): 'See' = cross-reference used for acronyms, synonyms, or alternate forms. AAVSO = initial letters of each word = acronym. Instructions explicitly say 'see' is used for acronyms = C. D (subentry) = wrong structure (AAVSO is its own heading, not listed under another).

quiz.question: [Q50 — Paraphrase: What does the asterisk (*) after 'moiré patterns* 13 341' mean?] Instructions: 'Where the title of an article appears as a sub-entry, as shown by an asterisk (*), the reader is encouraged to seek out the main entry under this title where further sub-entries, cross-references and references may be found.' Q: The asterisk in 'moiré patterns* 13 341' indicates ______. Your pick — D: indicates that moiré patterns is the most important of the subentries Correct — B: refers the reader to an entry whose title is moiré patterns Why does the asterisk signal 'go find the main entry' not 'most important'?
options: D is correct because asterisks often signal importance or priority in reference texts

The instructions explicitly define asterisk function: 'the reader is encouraged to seek out the MAIN ENTRY under this title'. Asterisk = 'there is a full main entry with this exact title elsewhere in the index'. B: 'refers the reader to an entry whose title is moiré patterns' = direct paraphrase of 'seek out the main entry under this title'. D (most important) is never mentioned in the instructions — it is a background knowledge assumption about asterisks that does NOT apply here.
B is wrong because the asterisk refers to aberration, not moiré patterns
D is correct because 13 341 shows moiré patterns has a larger volume reference
quiz.answer: ② Instructions explicitly define asterisk = 'seek out the main entry under this title' = B / D = assumed meaning from background knowledge, not from the instructions
quiz.explanation:
[Asterisk function per instructions]
'Where the title of an article appears as a sub-entry, as shown by an ASTERISK (*),
the reader is encouraged to seek out the MAIN ENTRY under this title'

= asterisk signals: this sub-entry title HAS a full main entry elsewhere
= the reader should go find that main entry

[Paraphrase chain → B]
'seek out the main entry under this title'
= go find the entry WHOSE TITLE is moiré patterns
= 'refers the reader to an entry whose title is moiré patterns' (B) ✓

[Why D (most important) fails]
'Most important of the subentries'
= not mentioned anywhere in the instructions
= this is BACKGROUND KNOWLEDGE about how asterisks work generally
= the instructions say: asterisk = directs to main entry, NOT 'marks importance'
= D = importing external assumption about asterisks ✗

[The anti-background-knowledge rule]
For instructions/reference text questions:
= ONLY use what the instructions SAY
= never apply general knowledge about symbol meanings
= the instructions define every symbol's specific function
= D's 'importance' meaning is general usage, not this index's definition
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q50): Instructions define symbol meanings exactly. Asterisk = 'seek out the main entry under this title' = refers reader to a main entry with that title = B. D = general asterisk meaning (importance) = background knowledge = never use when instructions define the symbol explicitly.

quiz.question: [Q52 — Paraphrase: What does 'see also Refractory' under Abrasive mean?] Instructions: 'References (using see also) are sometimes given at the end of an entry or a set of sub-entries to refer the reader to related topics.' Index: 'Abrasive... see also Refractory' Q: The inclusion of Refractory under Abrasive ______. Your pick — A: points to another section that will yield the same information Correct — D: points to another similar area of interest Why does 'see also' = related (not identical) and how does that eliminate A?
options: A is correct because see also directs readers to additional sources of the same information

'See also' function per instructions: 'refer the reader to RELATED TOPICS' (not identical topics). D: 'similar area of interest' = related topics = direct paraphrase. A: 'same information' = identical content = would require just 'see', not 'see also'. The distinction: 'see' = synonym/alternate form (same content), 'see also' = related but different area. Abrasive and Refractory are related industrial materials, not synonyms.
D is wrong because similar area could mean the same thing in different words
A is correct because Abrasive and Refractory share similar industrial applications
quiz.answer: ② 'See also' = related topics (instructions explicit) = D (similar area) / A = same information = would require 'see' not 'see also'
quiz.explanation:
['See' vs 'see also' distinction — critical]
'see' → used for: acronyms, synonyms, alternate forms
= directs to the SAME content in a different form
= effectively: 'go here instead'

'see also' → used for: related topics
= 'there is RELATED but DIFFERENT information over there'
= not the same content, but a similar area

[Paraphrase chain → D]
'see also' = refer to RELATED TOPICS
= Refractory = a related but distinct industrial material
= 'similar area of interest' (D) = related topics ✓

[Why A fails]
A: 'points to another section that will yield the SAME information'
= same information = synonyms/alternate forms
= that would require 'see' not 'see also'
= A confuses 'see' and 'see also' functions

[Even without knowing 'Refractory']
You don't need to know what refractory means
= the instruction tells you 'see also' = related topics
= related topics ≠ same information
= D (similar area) > A (same information) ✓

[Abrasive vs Refractory for context]
Abrasive = material used for grinding/polishing (e.g. sandpaper)
Refractory = material resistant to heat used in industrial furnaces
= related industrial materials but NOT the same
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q52): 'See also' = related topics (instructions say so explicitly). D = similar area of interest = related. A = same information = what 'see' (not 'see also') is for. Know the distinction: see = same/alternate form / see also = related but different. You don't need to know 'Refractory' — just apply 'see also' = related.

quiz.question:
[Unit 110 — Meta Pattern]

Q48: chose D (subentry) — subentry = wrong structure; instructions say 'see' = for acronyms = C
Q50: chose D (most important) — imported general asterisk meaning; instructions say asterisk = main entry reference
Q52: chose A (same information) — confused 'see' with 'see also'; instructions say 'see also' = related topics

All three share one error. Which correctly identifies it?
options: All three are vocabulary errors — unfamiliar words like acronym and refractory

All three = BACKGROUND KNOWLEDGE IMPORTATION: choosing based on general assumptions about symbol meanings ('asterisk = important', 'subentry = listed under', 'see also = same') instead of using the instructions' EXPLICIT DEFINITIONS. Fix: for reference/instructional texts, the instructions define EVERY symbol and term. Use only the definitions given, not background knowledge about how symbols work in other contexts.
All three are targeting errors — wrong instruction bullet point consulted
All three are paraphrasing errors about single words
quiz.answer: ② All three = imported background knowledge instead of using instructions' explicit definitions. Fix: instructions = the ONLY source for symbol meanings in this question type.
quiz.explanation:
[Q48: Background knowledge error]
Background knowledge: 'subentry' = secondary item under main item
Instruction says: 'see' = for acronyms, synonyms, alternate forms
AAVSO = initial letters = acronym → C (not D/subentry) ✓

[Q50: Background knowledge error]
Background knowledge: asterisk (*) often = important/primary/starred
Instruction says: asterisk = 'seek out the main entry under this title'
= = refers reader to main entry with that title = B ✓

[Q52: Background knowledge error]
Background knowledge: 'see also' = more of the same but maybe different angle
Instruction says: 'see also' = RELATED topics (not same information)
= different from 'see' which = synonyms/alternate forms
= D (similar area) not A (same information) ✓

[The core rule for reference text questions]
When questions refer to a document with EXPLICIT INSTRUCTIONS:
= treat the instructions as the ONLY VALID SOURCE for definitions
= do NOT use your background knowledge about how symbols generally work
= test each answer against the instruction text, not your assumptions

[Symbol function summary for Unit 110]
'see' = acronym/synonym/alternate form → same content elsewhere
'see also' = related topics → connected but different content
'*' (asterisk) = full main entry exists with this title → go find it
bold type = volume and page numbers
italics = names for objects, plants or animals (identification purposes)
quiz.action: Meta Rule (Unit 110): Reference/instructional texts define their own symbol system. ONLY use the instructions' definitions, never background knowledge about symbols. 'see' = acronym/synonym. 'see also' = related topics. '*' = main entry reference. Test every answer against the instruction text.

quiz.question: [Q71 — Targeting: How is Jude's son portrayed?] Passage: 'He was Age masquerading as Juvenility' — NOT a typical child 'doubly awake... regarding his companions' whole rounded lives rather than their immediate figures' = sees the whole picture of people's lives, not just the surface 'sitting passive' — does not join in Q: Jude's son is portrayed as ______. Your pick — B: vulnerable and endearing Correct — C: wise and self-sufficient Why does 'Age masquerading as Juvenility' eliminate B and confirm C?
options: B is correct because the boy is only ten years old and travelling alone for the first time

'Age masquerading as Juvenility' = ancient wisdom dressed in a child's body. 'Whole rounded lives' = he sees beyond surfaces to deep truths = WISE. 'Sitting passive' = he doesn't need others, observes from within himself = SELF-SUFFICIENT. B (vulnerable and endearing) = a typical child's traits — but the passage deliberately contrasts him AGAINST typical children. C precisely maps the two passage signals: ancient wisdom = wise / internal self-containment = self-sufficient.
C is wrong because self-sufficient children don't travel to meet absent parents
B is correct because the boy is described as enslaved and dwarfed, suggesting vulnerability
quiz.answer: ② 'Age masquerading as Juvenility' = ancient wisdom in young body = wise / 'sitting passive, whole rounded lives' = self-contained observation = self-sufficient
quiz.explanation:
['Age masquerading as Juvenility' decoded]
Age = ancient wisdom, maturity, accumulated experience
Juvenility = childlike appearance
masquerading = disguised as
= ancient wisdom wearing a child's mask
= NOT a typical vulnerable child
= the opposite of typical childhood ✗ (eliminates B)

['Whole rounded lives' decoded]
'regarding his companions... whole rounded lives rather than their immediate figures'
= he sees their ENTIRE life arc, not just how they look
= depth of perception beyond age = WISE ✓

['Sitting passive' decoded]
= he does not join the travellers who close their eyes
= he stays awake, internally self-contained
= does not need external engagement = SELF-SUFFICIENT ✓

[Why B (vulnerable and endearing) fails]
vulnerable = susceptible to harm, needing protection
endearing = lovably innocent, childlike
= these describe a TYPICAL 10-year-old's traits
= but the passage contrasts him AGAINST typical children
= 'even the kitten curled up... he remained just as before'
= he is NOT like the others = B is for the others, not him
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q71): 'Age masquerading as Juvenility' = the key signal. He is the OPPOSITE of a typical child. Test choices against this: B (vulnerable/endearing) = typical child traits = eliminated. C (wise/self-sufficient) = ancient wisdom + self-contained observation = matches the 'Age' signal.

quiz.question: [Q72 — Paraphrase: What effect does capitalising certain words have?] Capitalised words: 'Age', 'Juvenility', 'Divinity', 'Atlantic of Time' = ordinary concepts elevated to abstract universals Q: Capitalising certain words in this passage has the effect of ______. Your pick — C: emphasising by opposition the fact that this is no more than a little boy Correct — A: adding the solemnity and dignity of myth to the boy's character Why does capitalisation ELEVATE the boy rather than DIMINISH him?
options: C is correct because 'Age masquerading as Juvenility' contrasts ancient and young

Capitalising common nouns = ELEVATING them to mythic/universal status. 'Age', 'Divinity', 'Juvenility' capitalised = the boy IS associated with these grand, mythic concepts. A: 'solemnity and dignity of MYTH' = exactly what capitalisation achieves by treating ordinary words as proper nouns (like mythic figures). C: 'no more than a little boy' = DIMINISHES him — the opposite of what capitalisation does. Capitalisation = upward elevation, not downward reduction.
A is wrong because the boy is a real child, not a mythical figure
C is correct because the contrast between Age and Juvenility minimises the boy
quiz.answer: ② Capitalising common nouns = elevates them to mythic/universal status = adds solemnity and dignity (A) / C = diminishes ('no more than') = opposite direction
quiz.explanation:
[The effect of capitalising common nouns]
In literature, capitalising a common noun:
= transforms it into a proper noun with archetypal status
= it becomes a universal concept, not just an instance

Age (capitalised) = not just 'old age' but AGE as a cosmic force
Juvenility = not just 'youth' but JUVENILITY as a universal state
Divinity = not just 'godliness' but DIVINITY as a transcendent quality

= the boy is associated with these GRAND, MYTHIC forces
= he embodies universal archetypes, not just a typical child

[Paraphrase chain → A]
capitalised mythic concepts → boy gains MYTHIC STATURE
= 'solemnity and dignity of MYTH' added to his character ✓

[Why C is the opposite direction]
C: 'no more than a little boy'
= DIMINISHES him
= 'no more than' = minimising language
= capitalisation ELEVATES, not DIMINISHES
= C has the direction of the effect REVERSED ✗

[The 'opposition' in C is the trap]
C mentions 'emphasising by opposition'
= Hardy does use contrast (Age vs Juvenility)
= but the effect of capitalisation is elevation, not reduction
= the contrast makes the boy MORE remarkable, not less
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q72): Capitalisation of common nouns = upward elevation to mythic/universal status. A = adds solemnity and dignity of myth = upward. C = 'no more than a little boy' = downward/diminishing = wrong direction. Effect direction matters: capitalisation always elevates.

quiz.question: [Q74 — Targeting: What impression does the narrator give of Jude's son?] Passage evidence: 'Age masquerading as Juvenility... doing it SO BADLY that his real self showed through crevices' = ancient self barely contained in a child's body 'enslaved and dwarfed Divinity' = divine nature = imprisoned and made small Q: The narrator gives the impression that Jude's son ______. Your pick — C: cannot understand the world and so withdraws from it Correct — A: feels constrained within a boy's body Why does 'real self showed through crevices' + 'dwarfed' = A not C?
options: C is correct because the boy sits passively and does not engage with the travellers

'Cannot understand' would make the boy LESS than others. But 'Age masquerading as Juvenility... real self showed through CREVICES' = his ancient/larger self is leaking through the cracks of his child body = he EXCEEDS the body, not fails to understand. 'Enslaved and dwarfed Divinity' = his divine nature is imprisoned and made small by the physical form. A: 'constrained within a boy's body' = the body LIMITS him = direct paraphrase of 'dwarfed' + 'enslaved' + 'real self cracking through'.
A is wrong because the boy is physically comfortable in his body
C is correct because he doesn't join the other travellers showing withdrawal from the world
quiz.answer: ② 'Real self showed through crevices' + 'enslaved and dwarfed Divinity' = his nature is TOO LARGE for the body = constrained (A) / C = misreads passivity as incomprehension
quiz.explanation:
['Real self showed through crevices' decoded]
= his true nature is bigger than his child's body
= it SEEPS THROUGH the gaps (crevices) in the child exterior
= the body is too small to contain what he IS
= he is CONSTRAINED by the physical form ✓

['Enslaved and dwarfed Divinity' decoded]
enslaved = trapped, imprisoned, not free
dwarfed = made smaller than natural size
Divinity = his true cosmic/divine nature
= his divine nature is imprisoned AND minimised by his child's body
= CONSTRAINED within a boy's body ✓

[Why A = constrained in body]
All evidence points to: his true self is too big for the body
= the body CONSTRAINS him
= A: 'feels constrained within a boy's body' ✓

[Why C fails]
C: 'cannot understand the world and so withdraws'
= implies the boy is LIMITED IN PERCEPTION
= but he actually SEES TOO MUCH (whole rounded lives, Atlantic of Time)
= he doesn't withdraw from failure to understand
= he sits passively because he already perceives everything
= passivity ≠ withdrawal from incomprehension
= C misreads the cause of his passivity ✗
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q74): 'Real self showed through crevices' + 'enslaved and dwarfed' = his nature is TOO LARGE for the body = constrained (A). C assumes passivity = failure to understand. But he sits passively because he perceives TOO MUCH, not too little. His passivity = transcendence, not incomprehension.

quiz.question:
[Q75 — Paraphrase: Which quotation conveys the MELANCHOLIC side of the boy's nature?]

Melancholic = a deep, heavy sadness; a sombre, brooding quality; longing or sorrow

A: 'He then seemed to be doubly awake' — heightened perception
B: 'like an enslaved and dwarfed Divinity' — physical constraint, imprisonment
C: 'He was Age masquerading as Juvenility' — paradoxical identity
D: 'his face took a back view over some great Atlantic of Time' — dark, vast temporal imagery

Q: Which conveys the melancholic side?

Your pick — B
Correct — D

How does 'great Atlantic of Time' evoke melancholy while 'enslaved and dwarfed' evokes something different?
options: B is correct because being enslaved and dwarfed evokes sadness and suffering

'Enslaved and dwarfed Divinity' = constraint and limitation = OPPRESSION or FRUSTRATION but not the deep sorrowful longing of melancholy. D: 'face took a BACK VIEW over some great ATLANTIC OF TIME' = gazing backward over an immense, dark, endless expanse of time = MELANCHOLIC imagery: brooding over the vastness of time, a sense of ancient sorrow and weight. The 'back view' = looking backward = nostalgic/retrospective sadness. 'Atlantic of Time' = dark, immeasurable, lonely = the emotional register of melancholy.
D is wrong because the Atlantic of Time refers to his recent travel by ship
B is correct because being enslaved makes someone deeply sad
quiz.answer: ② B = constraint/oppression (not melancholy) / D = backward gaze over vast dark temporal expanse = the imagery of brooding sorrowful melancholy
quiz.explanation:
[Melancholy decoded]
Melancholic = deep, heavy sadness; brooding quality; sorrow about the vastness of time/existence
= not anger, not frustration, not constraint
= the feeling of being small before something immense and irreversible

[Testing D: 'great Atlantic of Time']
'face took a BACK VIEW'
= he looks backwards = retrospective, wistful, nostalgic

'some great ATLANTIC of Time'
= Atlantic = vast, dark, endless ocean
= Time = immeasurable
= the boy gazes backward over an incomprehensible expanse of time
= the scale = oppressive, immense, melancholic
= this is the emotional register of MELANCHOLY ✓

[Testing B: 'enslaved and dwarfed Divinity']
Enslaved = imprisoned, trapped
Dwarfed = made small
= his divine nature is constrained
= emotional register: FRUSTRATION, OPPRESSION, LIMITATION
= not the brooding backward-looking sadness of melancholy
= B = constraint/limitation, not deep temporal sorrow ✗

[A and C also considered]
A: 'doubly awake' = heightened perception = alertness, not sadness
C: 'Age masquerading as Juvenility' = paradoxical identity = observation, not sadness

[The BACK VIEW = key signal]
= looking backward = the quintessential melancholic gesture
= combined with ATLANTIC of TIME = overwhelming temporal scale
= D = melancholy ✓
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q75): Melancholic = brooding backward-looking sorrow about vastness and time. D = 'back view over great Atlantic of Time' = backward gaze + dark immeasurable temporal expanse = melancholy. B = 'enslaved and dwarfed' = constraint/oppression = different emotion (frustration). Match the SPECIFIC emotional register, not just 'negative'.

source: Unit 109 — Meta: Contra-typical + Direction + Emotional Register

quiz.question:
[Unit 109 — Meta Pattern]

Q71: chose B (vulnerable/endearing) — typical child traits, but this boy is contra-typical
Q72: chose C (no more than a boy) — capitalisation ELEVATES; C DIMINISHES = wrong direction
Q74: chose C (cannot understand) — passivity = transcendence not incomprehension
Q75: chose B (enslaved/dwarfed) — constraint not melancholy; D = backward-gaze = temporal sorrow

Three correction tools. Which correctly identifies them?
options: All four are vocabulary errors — Hardy's archaic language was unfamiliar

Tool 1 = CONTRA-TYPICAL SIGNAL (Q71): 'Age masquerading as Juvenility' = he is the OPPOSITE of a typical child. Reject choices describing typical child traits (B). Tool 2 = DIRECTION OF EFFECT (Q72): capitalisation = upward elevation to mythic status. C = downward ('no more than') = wrong direction. Always identify: does this device ELEVATE or DIMINISH? Tool 3 = EMOTIONAL REGISTER PRECISION (Q74, Q75): passivity ≠ incomprehension (Q74 = too much perception); enslaved ≠ melancholy (Q75 = constraint/frustration ≠ temporal sorrow).
All four are targeting errors — wrong paragraph analysed
All four are paraphrasing errors only
quiz.answer: ② Contra-typical signal + direction of effect + emotional register precision — three tools
quiz.explanation:
[Tool 1: Contra-typical signal (Q71)]
'Age masquerading as Juvenility'
= explicitly signals: NOT a typical child
= reject all answers describing typical child traits
= B (vulnerable/endearing) = typical child = eliminated
= C (wise/self-sufficient) = unusual, not-typical = correct

[Tool 2: Direction of effect (Q72)]
Capitalisation = ELEVATES to mythic/universal status
A = adds solemnity/dignity = upward ✓
C = 'no more than' = downward/diminishing = wrong direction
Fix: identify the DIRECTION of the literary device before choosing

[Tool 3: Emotional register precision (Q74, Q75)]
Q74: passivity = he perceives TOO MUCH (not too little)
= not 'cannot understand' but 'has already grasped everything'
= constraint (A) = body too small for his perception

Q75: enslaved/dwarfed = CONSTRAINT/OPPRESSION register
melancholy = BROODING TEMPORAL SORROW register
= different emotions: frustration/limitation ≠ deep backward-looking sadness
= D = backward gaze over Atlantic of Time = melancholy's specific register

[Hardy's technique throughout]
He builds a paradox: cosmic/ancient soul trapped in a 10-year-old's body
= the body CONSTRAINS the soul (not 'cannot understand')
= the soul looks backward over eternity (melancholy)
= the soul is ELEVATED by mythic language (capitalisation)
quiz.action:
Meta Rule (Unit 109):
Contra-typical signal: 'Age masquerading' = not a typical child → reject typical child traits.
Direction of effect: capitalisation = upward elevation; 'no more than' = downward → wrong direction.
Emotional register: passivity ≠ incomprehension (he sees too much). Enslaved/dwarfed ≠ melancholy (different emotions). Match the specific emotional register.

quiz.question: [Q79 — Paraphrase: What is the COMMON PERCEPTION of Napoleon?] Passage (lines 1–3): '...many theatrical and histrionic touches, that have led people to speak of him as 'nothing but' an actor...' Q: A common perception of Napoleon Bonaparte is that he was a ______. Your pick — A: creative artist Correct — D: hollow showman Why does 'nothing but an actor' = hollow showman, NOT creative artist?
options: A is correct because the passage describes Napoleon as an 'artist manqué'

'Nothing but an actor' = dismissive = ALL PERFORMANCE, NO substance. 'Hollow showman' = exactly this: hollow = empty inside, showman = performer for effect. 'Creative artist' = the AUTHOR's positive reassessment ('artist manqué') — not the public's dismissive common perception. The question asks for COMMON perception = dismissive public view = D.
D is wrong because a showman has real skills that Napoleon demonstrated in battle
A is correct because theatrical and histrionic confirm artistic creativity
quiz.answer: ② 'nothing but an actor' = dismissive = hollow (no substance) showman (all performance) = D / 'artist manqué' = AUTHOR's view, not common perception
quiz.explanation:
['Nothing but an actor' decoded]
'nothing but' = ONLY this, nothing more (dismissive)
= all surface, no real substance

[D = hollow showman]
holllow = empty, no real substance beneath the performance
showman = person who performs for effect
= D: direct paraphrase of 'nothing but an actor' ✓

[Source distinction]
Common perception (public): 'nothing but an actor' → D (hollow showman)
Author's own view: 'artist manqué' → sympathetic, complex
= the question asks for COMMON perception
= A (creative artist) = author's view, not public's = wrong source ✗
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q79): 'Common perception' ≠ 'author's view'. Public says 'nothing but an actor' = hollow showman (D). Author says 'artist manqué' = sympathetic. Always check: whose view does the question ask for?

quiz.question: [Q80 — Paraphrase: To 'magic a dry score' (line 5) means to ______] Passage (lines 4–5): 'His great memory for facts was transmuted by the imagination just as a great orchestral conductor 'magics' a dry score.' dry score = lifeless sheet music / conductor 'magics' = brings to life Q: To magic a dry score means to ______. Your pick — B: give a score the illusion of substance Correct — A: infuse a score with richness and vigour Why 'genuine infusion' (A) not 'illusion' (B)?
options: B is correct because a conductor creates the illusion of music from silent marks on paper

'Transmuted' = genuinely transformed, not illusory. The conductor draws out the REAL potential of the score — the music was already in the notes. A: 'infuse with richness and vigour' = real enrichment. B: 'illusion of substance' = fake substance. Genuine transformation ≠ creating an illusion.
A is wrong because richness and vigour are not mentioned in the passage
B is correct because 'dry' implies the score lacks real substance
quiz.answer: ② 'Transmuted' = genuine real transformation / conductor activates REAL potential = infuses with richness (A) / B = illusion = fake = contradicts 'transmuted'
quiz.explanation:
[The conductor metaphor]
dry score = has potential but is static on paper
conductor 'magics' = genuinely ACTIVATES that potential
= the music was always there — conductor makes it LIVE

[A = genuine infusion]
'Infuse with richness and vigour' = real enrichment ✓

[B = wrong direction]
'Illusion of substance' = fake richness
'Transmuted' = real transformation, not deception
= B contradicts 'transmuted' ✗
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q80): 'Transmuted' = genuine transformation. Conductor genuinely enriches (A), not fakes (B). Genuine transformation ≠ illusion.

quiz.question: [Q82 — Paraphrase: 'apology' (line 11) closest in meaning to ______] Passage (lines 10–12): 'his own apology: "I am ambitious... my ambition is so intimately allied to my whole being that it CANNOT BE SEPARATED from it."' Q: 'Apology' is closest in meaning to ______. Your pick — D: an excuse for a self-acknowledged fault Correct — C: a defence of one's natural tendencies How does 'cannot be separated from my whole being' reveal defence, not excuse?
options: D is correct because Napoleon acknowledges he is called ambitious and tries to excuse it

D = excuse for a fault = admitting wrongdoing + seeking forgiveness. But Napoleon says ambition 'CANNOT BE SEPARATED from my whole being' = it IS his nature, not a fault to excuse. He is DEFENDING his nature as inseparable from himself. Classical 'apology' = formal defence speech (Socrates' Apology = his defence, not his sorry). C = defence of natural tendencies ✓
C is wrong because Napoleon admits 'this is an error' showing he acknowledges a fault
D is correct because Napoleon knows he was wrong to be ambitious
quiz.answer: ② Napoleon does NOT admit a fault — he says ambition is inseparable from his nature = DEFENDS natural tendencies (C) / classical 'apology' = formal defence, not sorry
quiz.explanation:
[Napoleon's statement decoded]
'this is an error' = HE SAYS the accusation of ambition is the error
= he disputes the fault, not admits it

'cannot be separated from my whole being'
= ambition = his nature = not a fault but his identity
= DEFENDING his natural tendency

[Classical 'apology']
= formal defence speech
= Socrates' Apology = his trial defence
= Napoleon's apology = his formal defence of his nature
= C: defence of natural tendencies ✓

D: excuse = admits fault + seeks forgiveness = Napoleon does neither ✗
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q82): Classical 'apology' = formal defence (not 'sorry'). Napoleon defends his nature as inseparable = C. D requires admitting a fault — Napoleon says the accusation itself is the error.

quiz.question: [Q83 — Paraphrase: Author's attitude toward Napoleon?] Author tone signals: • 'a truer assessment would be to say...' = corrects/defends Napoleon • 'artist manqué' = sympathetic framing • explores 'dual man' theory with depth = intellectually engaged Q: The author's attitude is ______. Your pick — D: He finds the polarised nature disturbing Correct — B: He empathises with Napoleon and finds his complexities fascinating
options: D is correct because the author presents Napoleon as both theatrical and genuine — a disturbing contradiction

'A truer assessment' = author DEFENDS Napoleon = empathy. 'Artist manqué' = sympathetic framing = empathy. Deep exploration of 'dual man' theory = intellectual fascination. B = empathises + finds fascinating ✓. D = disturbing = the author would distance himself — he does the opposite (defends and engages deeply).
B is wrong because the author ultimately calls him 'nothing but an actor'
D is correct because the author cannot reconcile the different aspects of Napoleon's character
quiz.answer: ② Author defends Napoleon ('truer assessment') + explores complexity with depth = empathises + fascinating (B) / D = disturbing requires distancing — author does opposite
quiz.explanation:
[Author's stance markers]
'A TRUER ASSESSMENT' = corrects the dismissive public view = defends Napoleon ✓
'artist manqué' = sympathetic artistic framing ✓
Deep exploration of 'dual man' = fascinated engagement ✓

[B = empathises + fascinating]
Empathy = defends against dismissal
Fascinating = engaged deeply with complexity
= B ✓

[D = disturbing]
