disturbing = unsettling, want to distance
= if disturbed, would NOT spend so much time defending and exploring
= opposite of what the author does ✗
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q83): Quality of engagement reveals attitude. Author defends (empathy) and explores deeply (fascination) = B. Disturbing = distancing — author engages the opposite way.

source: Unit 107 — Meta: Source + Genuine vs Illusion + Classical Vocab

quiz.question:
[Unit 107 — Meta Pattern]

Q79: chose A (creative artist) — author's view / question asked for COMMON perception
Q80: chose B (illusion) — transmuted = genuine, not illusory
Q82: chose D (excuse for fault) — classical apology = defence, not sorry
Q83: chose D (disturbing) — deep engagement = fascination not disturbance

Three tools. Which correctly identifies them?
options: All four are vocabulary errors

Tool 1 = SOURCE DISTINCTION (Q79, Q83): whose view does the question ask for? Common perception ≠ author's view. Tool 2 = GENUINE vs ILLUSION (Q80): 'transmuted' = real transformation. A = real richness. B = fake illusion = contradicts transmuted. Tool 3 = CLASSICAL VOCABULARY (Q82): 'apology' = formal defence speech (not sorry). Napoleon defends his nature, does not apologise for a fault.
All four are targeting errors
All four are paraphrasing errors only
quiz.answer: ② Source distinction + genuine vs illusion + classical vocabulary
quiz.explanation:
[Tool 1: Source distinction]
Q79: common perception (public) = 'nothing but an actor' = hollow showman
Q83: author's view = empathises + fascinating
Always identify whose view the question asks for

[Tool 2: Genuine vs illusion]
'Transmuted' = genuine transformation
Conductor genuinely activates real potential
= A (real richness) not B (fake illusion)

[Tool 3: Classical vocabulary]
'Apology' in classical use = formal defence speech
Napoleon defends his nature = C
D = modern colloquial 'sorry' = wrong register
quiz.action: Meta Rule (Unit 107): Source distinction / Genuine vs illusion / Classical vocabulary. Three tools for high literary texts.

quiz.question: [Q54 — Paraphrase: Safety pin through cheek/earlobe = expression of wearer's ______] Passage (lines 11–18): 'aspects of the Punk Look appealed... for the love and care that we give to very small children, especially to injured ones' 'those pale scratched faces and scraped knees... ill-fitting, often half-undone jackets' 'for that Punk trademark... the outsize safety pin... it could not help but remind every mother of the awful moment when she ran one just like it into her darling instead of into his nappy.' Q: The safety pin is an expression of the wearer's ______. Your pick — C: carelessness Correct — D: vulnerability How does the 'nappy/baby' image make vulnerability the correct reading?
options: C is correct because wearing a safety pin through skin suggests reckless disregard for pain

The safety pin reminds mothers of accidentally pricking their BABY (darling/nappy = infant care context). Babies = the ultimate symbol of vulnerability and helplessness. The Punk Look references 'injured ones', 'scraped knees', 'scratched faces' = damaged and unprotected children. D: vulnerability = being exposed and unprotected like an injured child. C: carelessness = the wearer doesn't care = but the context is about what was done TO them (neglect), not their own attitude.
D is wrong because the safety pin is designed to be aggressive and threatening
C is correct because the safety pin shows the wearer ignores safety conventions
quiz.answer: ② Safety pin → baby/nappy → injured infant = vulnerability. The Punk Look channels the image of an unprotected child, not carelessness about self.
quiz.explanation:
[The nappy/baby connection]
'remind every mother of the awful moment when she ran one just like it into her DARLING instead of into his NAPPY'
= safety pin = accidental injury TO A BABY
= nappy = infant = ultimate vulnerable being

[Context of vulnerability]
'love and care... for very small children, especially INJURED ones'
'pale scratched faces and scraped knees' = hurt, unprotected
'fluffy Easter-chick hair' = baby-like softness
= the Punk Look channels the imagery of an unprotected, injured child

[Paraphrase chain → D]
baby imagery + injured children + scratched/scraped = VULNERABILITY
= the wearer presents as unprotected, exposed, potentially harmed
= D: vulnerability ✓

[Why C (carelessness) fails]
Carelessness = the person doesn't care / is reckless
= but the context is about WHAT SOCIETY FAILED TO DO for these children
= the neglect was done BY OTHERS (callous teachers, immature parents)
= the safety pin = symbol of a wound inflicted on the vulnerable
= NOT: 'I don't care about safety'
= C gets the direction of the carelessness wrong ✗
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q54): Safety pin → nappy/baby → injured infant = vulnerability. The Punk Look channels unprotected/injured child imagery (D). Carelessness (C) reverses the direction — the neglect was done TO them, not by them.

quiz.question: [Q55 — Targeting: Author's PREDOMINANT ATTITUDE toward Punk adopters] Two different emotional registers in the passage: ① What PEOPLE IN BRITAIN felt (line 20): 'rage, guilt, COMPASSION and fear' ② What the AUTHOR (Alison Lurie) presents: 'demand for attention', 'callous/helpless' parents, 'miserable baby' Q: The writer's predominant attitude toward those who adopted the Punk style is most akin to ______. Your pick — A: rage Correct — C: compassion Why does the author's framing reveal compassion, not rage?
options: A is correct because the passage describes the Punk Look as intended to offend and frighten

Rage = what some PEOPLE IN BRITAIN felt. The AUTHOR's framing: Punk adopters are children demanding attention from 'parents too immature or exhausted', 'callous teachers', a 'welfare state that seemed uninterested'. She explains THEIR PAIN and the social neglect behind it — this is COMPASSION (understanding/empathising with their suffering). Rage would mean the author judges them negatively. She judges the SOCIETY that failed them.
C is wrong because Lurie describes the Look as 'disturbing' showing she disapproves
A is correct because she says the Look was 'viciously angry' showing she shares the anger
quiz.answer: ② A = what some people felt (public emotion) / Author's framing = explains the social neglect that caused Punk = understands their pain = COMPASSION (C)
quiz.explanation:
[The crucial distinction]
Question: WRITER's attitude
= not: what British people felt
= not: what the Punk wearers felt
= what ALISON LURIE thinks about the Punk adopters

[Author's framing of Punk adopters]
'In the language of clothes, the Punk style was a DEMAND FOR ATTENTION'
= she reads it as a cry for help, not mere aggression

'against those who should have paid attention to these kids in the past:
parents who were too immature or too exhausted;
callous or helpless teachers and social workers'
= she explains the CAUSE: they were failed by society
= she attributes blame to the SOCIETY, not to the Punk wearers

[The 'miserable baby' reading]
'a viciously angry, MISERABLE BABY'
= miserable = suffering, in pain
= she sees them as suffering children demanding to be seen
= COMPASSION for their pain ✓

[Why A (rage) is wrong]
Rage is listed among what 'people in Britain' felt (line 20)
= this is the PUBLIC'S reaction
= the AUTHOR does not rage at Punk wearers
= she explains and interprets their pain = compassion
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q55): Separate AUTHOR's attitude from PUBLIC's reaction. Public felt rage/guilt/fear. Author explains the social neglect behind Punk = understands their pain = compassion (C). Rage = what she describes others feeling, not what she herself feels toward them.

quiz.question: [Q56 — Logic: The passage supports which view of clothing styles?] Passage opening: 'In the LANGUAGE OF CLOTHES, the Punk style was a DEMAND for attention' = clothes = language = communication of something deeper Passage content: Punk = cry of rage, political protest, social commentary, expression of parental neglect Correct — B: expressions of profound individual and social attitudes How does 'language of clothes' + the whole passage support B over the other choices?
options: B is correct because clothing communicates deep individual and social meaning

'In the LANGUAGE OF CLOTHES' = opening metaphor = clothes COMMUNICATE something profound. The Punk Look communicates: individual pain (neglected children), social critique (failed by parents/welfare), political attitude (protest toward action). B: 'expressions of profound individual AND social attitudes' = directly paraphrases 'language of clothes' + the whole passage's argument. The other choices miss the 'language/communication' metaphor that frames everything.
C is correct because Punk was designed to disguise real feelings behind aggressive appearance
D is correct because Punk emerged in response to changing social aesthetics
quiz.answer: ① 'Language of clothes' = clothes communicate profound meaning / Punk = individual pain + social critique + political protest = profound individual and social attitudes = B
quiz.explanation:
['Language of clothes' opening]
The FIRST WORDS: 'In the language of clothes'
= the author's thesis: clothing = a form of language
= language expresses meaning, attitudes, messages
= therefore clothing expresses PROFOUND ATTITUDES

[What Punk communicates per the passage]
Individual attitudes: demand for attention, miserable baby, rage of neglect
Social attitudes: critique of callous parents, welfare state, social workers
Political: 'fashion moving toward political protest, possibly toward political action'

= PROFOUND INDIVIDUAL AND SOCIAL ATTITUDES = B ✓

[Why other choices fail]
A: 'creation of capitalism' = never mentioned
C: 'disguise essential feelings' = opposite of what Punk does (it REVEALS, not disguises)
D: 'art forms of taste and aesthetics' = too narrow; Punk is about attitudes, not aesthetics

[The 'language' metaphor is the key]
If clothes = language → they COMMUNICATE
Communication of profound attitudes = B
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q56): 'Language of clothes' = opening thesis = clothing communicates profound attitudes. B = 'expressions of profound individual and social attitudes' = direct paraphrase of the passage's entire argument. C reverses the direction (reveals, not disguises). The opening metaphor frames the whole passage.

source: Unit 106 — Meta: Author vs Public + Image Chain

quiz.question:
[Unit 106 — Meta Pattern]

Q54: chose C (carelessness) — direction wrong: neglect was done TO them, not by them / baby = vulnerability
Q55: chose A (rage) — mixed up AUTHOR's attitude with PUBLIC's reaction

Two correction tools. Which correctly identifies them?
options: Both are vocabulary errors — unfamiliar words

Tool 1 = IMAGE CHAIN TRACING (Q54): safety pin → nappy → baby → injured infant = vulnerability. Don't read the symbol directly (pin = aggressive) — trace the image chain the author builds (pin → baby accident → unprotected child). Tool 2 = AUTHOR vs PUBLIC SEPARATION (Q55): passage lists what 'people in Britain felt' (rage/guilt/fear/compassion) AND what the author thinks. These are different. Author's framing = explains social neglect = compassion for Punk wearers. Never conflate the two.
Both are targeting errors — wrong paragraph
Both are paraphrasing errors only
quiz.answer: ② Image chain tracing (Q54: pin→baby→vulnerability) + author vs public separation (Q55: author=compassion, public=rage)
quiz.explanation:
[Tool 1: Image chain tracing (Q54)]
Surface reading: safety pin = aggressive/sharp = carelessness or aggression
Image chain the author builds:
pin → reminds mothers → pricking their darling → nappy (baby)
= baby context → VULNERABILITY

Fix: trace the metaphorical chain, not the surface meaning
The author deliberately connects pin to baby = vulnerability signal

[Tool 2: Author vs public separation (Q55)]
Passage structure: describes public reaction AND implies author's interpretation

'People in Britain felt RAGE, guilt, compassion and fear'
= listing public emotional reactions

Author's framing: explains the social neglect that caused Punk
= she understands their pain = her attitude = COMPASSION

Fix: identify WHOSE emotion is described
= public emotion ≠ author's attitude
= question asks for AUTHOR's predominant attitude
quiz.action:
Meta Rule (Unit 106):
Image chain: trace the metaphor the author builds (pin→baby→vulnerability) not the surface meaning.
Author vs public: always identify whose emotion/attitude is described. The author's framing and the public's reaction are different things.

quiz.question: [Q97 — Paraphrase: How does modern psychiatry deal with society's problems?] Passage (lines 5–7): 'It helps society REPRESS unpleasant, embarrassing facts — economic problems, political problems, religious conflicts, and so forth. It SANITISES the state. It's a GARBAGE DISPOSAL procedure.' Q: Modern psychiatry deals with the problems of society by ______. Your pick — C: analysing them Correct — D: hiding them Why do 'repress', 'sanitise', 'garbage disposal' all paraphrase 'hiding'?
options: C is correct because psychiatry analyses mental conditions caused by social problems

All three passage words mean removing/concealing, not understanding: REPRESS = suppress, push down and out of sight. SANITISE = clean up (remove the 'dirty' reality). GARBAGE DISPOSAL = throw away what is unwanted. None of these words involve analysis or understanding. D: 'hiding them' = making them disappear from view = direct paraphrase of all three. C (analysing) = the opposite — analysis requires confronting and examining, not removing.
D is wrong because psychiatry does bring social problems to public attention
C is correct because Freud originally intended psychiatry for analysis
quiz.answer: ② repress + sanitise + garbage disposal = all mean REMOVING FROM SIGHT = hiding (D) / analysing (C) = confronting and examining = opposite direction
quiz.explanation:
[Three synonyms all pointing to 'hiding']
REPRESS = push down, suppress, remove from conscious awareness
SANITISE = clean up, make look acceptable by removing the unpleasant
GARBAGE DISPOSAL = throw away what is unwanted

= all three = REMOVE PROBLEMS FROM SIGHT, not address them

[Paraphrase chain → D]
repress + sanitise + garbage disposal
= making problems disappear from view
= HIDING THEM ✓

[Why C (analysing) is the opposite]
Analysis = examining, understanding, bringing to light
= you cannot repress/sanitise while analysing
= Szasz's whole argument: psychiatry should analyse (Freud's intention)
= but MODERN psychiatry does the opposite = hides = D

[Szasz's core argument]
Freud: psychiatry should UNDO repression (reveal/analyse)
Modern psychiatry: IS the agent of repression (hides)
= C describes what psychiatry SHOULD do / D describes what it ACTUALLY does
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q97): Three passage words (repress, sanitise, garbage disposal) all mean removing from sight = hiding (D). C (analysing) = confronting = the opposite. Szasz criticises modern psychiatry for hiding, not analysing, social problems.

quiz.question: [Q98 — Paraphrase: 'It helps society repress unpleasant, embarrassing facts' is consistent with ______] Passage argument: Szasz: psychiatry labels social problems (economic/political/religious conflicts) as mental illness. This means: instead of addressing ROOT CAUSES → treats individual as the 'patient' = addresses the SYMPTOM (the person's behaviour) not the CAUSE (the social structure) Q: This type of repression is consistent with ______. Your pick — C: the rectification of social problems Correct — A: treating the symptoms, rather than the causes, of mental illness Why does 'rectification' (C) describe the opposite of what Szasz says?
options: C is correct because psychiatry does attempt to correct social problems at the individual level

Rectification = correcting/fixing the problem itself. But Szasz says psychiatry DISGUISES social problems as mental illness, treating the person (symptom) instead of the economic/political cause. A: 'treating SYMPTOMS not CAUSES' = the person's behaviour = the visible symptom / the social structure = the real cause. C: rectification = actually fixing the social problem. Szasz says psychiatry does NOT fix social problems — it hides them. C = positive/solution-oriented = wrong.
A is wrong because mental illness has no clear 'cause' in Szasz's view
C is correct because psychiatry does make social corrections over time
quiz.answer: ② C = rectification = actually fixing the problem = NOT what Szasz says / A = treating symptoms not causes = psychiatry addresses the person (visible) not the society (root) = correct
quiz.explanation:
[Szasz's argument decoded]
Social problems (economic/political) = ROOT CAUSES
Person's behaviour or distress = VISIBLE SYMPTOM

Psychiatry does: labels the person as mentally ill
= treats the symptom (the person) ✓
= IGNORES the cause (the social structure) ✓

[Paraphrase chain → A]
'helps society repress unpleasant facts' =
= society's embarrassing problems (causes) are hidden
= only the individual (symptom) is treated
= A: 'treating SYMPTOMS rather than CAUSES' ✓

[Why C fails]
Rectification = correction = actually fixing
= if psychiatry RECTIFIED social problems
= the problems would be solved
= but Szasz says they are REPRESSED (not solved)
= C is the opposite of what Szasz describes

['Rectification' positive connotation trap]
Rectification = positive word (improvement, correction)
= Szasz's whole argument = psychiatry does NOT improve things
= it merely hides them
= positive connotation words = eliminate for Szasz
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q98): Szasz says psychiatry hides social problems (not fixes them). A = symptoms not causes = correct (person treated, not society). C = rectification = actually fixing = the opposite of hiding. Positive solution words (rectification) = eliminated when Szasz's argument is about concealment.

quiz.question: [Q99 — Paraphrase: Psychiatry is like primitive religion because it ______] Passage (lines 7–11): 'If there is a bad harvest, it's because we haven't prayed enough or because we ate the wrong food. It isn't because we didn't FERTILISE THE SOIL or because we are STUPID AND DON'T KNOW HOW TO FARM PROPERLY; it's some INTERVENTION OF A DEITY which we can placate by a prayer.' Primitive religion: deflects blame from human error → to supernatural cause Q: Psychiatry is like primitive religion in that it ______. Your pick — A: places human misfortunes in perspective Correct — D: discourages society from accepting liability for the condition of the world Why does the farming/deity analogy specifically demonstrate D?
options: A is correct because both psychiatry and religion help people contextualise suffering

The analogy: real cause = 'we didn't fertilise the soil' (human error/responsibility). Attributed cause = 'intervention of a deity' (supernatural). By blaming the deity, people AVOID accepting liability for bad farming = they escape accountability. Psychiatry does the same: real cause = social/economic problems. Attributed cause = mental illness. By labelling problems as mental illness, society avoids accepting liability for the conditions that created the problem. D = discourages LIABILITY acceptance = direct paraphrase of the analogy.
D is wrong because the passage says 'we are stupid' showing individual blame
A is correct because the analogy shows how religion contextualises a bad harvest
quiz.answer: ② The analogy: blame deity (not farming error) = avoid liability for bad farming / psychiatry: blame mental illness (not social causes) = society avoids liability = D
quiz.explanation:
[The farming analogy decoded]
Real cause of bad harvest:
= 'didn't fertilise the soil' / 'stupid and don't know how to farm properly'
= HUMAN ERROR = human RESPONSIBILITY/LIABILITY

What they say instead:
= 'some intervention of a deity' / 'haven't prayed enough'
= SUPERNATURAL CAUSE = NOT our fault

= By attributing to deity → they ESCAPE LIABILITY for their own farming mistakes

[Psychiatry parallel]
Real cause of social problems:
= economic inequality, political failures, social neglect
= HUMAN/SOCIAL RESPONSIBILITY

What psychiatry says instead:
= it's a mental illness
= MEDICAL/DISEASE CAUSE = not society's fault

= By attributing to illness → society ESCAPES LIABILITY for the conditions it created

[Paraphrase chain → D]
deflecting blame from human error → to supernatural/medical cause
= DISCOURAGES accepting liability
= D ✓

[Why A (places in perspective) fails]
'Perspective' = contextualises, helps understand
= too gentle/positive a reading
= the analogy is about BLAME DEFLECTION, not perspective-giving
= A describes a neutral/helpful function; Szasz is making a CRITICAL argument
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q99): The farming/deity analogy = deflect blame from human error to supernatural = avoid liability. Psychiatry: deflect from social causes to mental illness = society avoids liability. D = discourages accepting liability = direct match. A = 'perspective' = too positive/neutral for Szasz's critical argument.

quiz.question: [Q100 — Targeting: What does Szasz IMPLY is the best way to treat human problems?] Szasz's criticism logic: Psychiatry does: deflects responsibility onto 'mental illness' / 'deity' / external cause Szasz criticises this: because it avoids confronting real human causes and responsibilities Therefore Szasz implies: the best approach is the OPPOSITE of deflection = acknowledge our OWN responsibility for problems Q: Human problems are best treated by acknowledging ______. Your pick — C: that they are often beyond our control Correct — B: our responsibility for them Why does C describe EXACTLY what Szasz criticises, making it the wrong answer?
options: C is correct because Szasz says even primitive religion recognised problems as beyond human control

C: 'beyond our control' = exactly what psychiatry DOES (labels problems as illness/external cause) = what Szasz CRITICISES. B: 'our responsibility' = what the farmer should have accepted ('I didn't fertilise properly') instead of blaming the deity. Szasz's implied ideal: accept that bad harvests happen because of OUR farming decisions = accept liability. Human problems best treated by accepting we caused them and can address them.
B is wrong because Szasz doesn't say we should feel guilty for social problems
C is correct because Szasz says we should be humble about the limits of human understanding
quiz.answer: ② C = 'beyond our control' = exactly what psychiatry/religion does (deflect) = what Szasz criticises / B = 'our responsibility' = what the farmer should accept = Szasz's implied ideal
quiz.explanation:
[The reverse logic for 'implies' questions]
Szasz CRITICISES: psychiatry deflects responsibility → says problems are external
= 'beyond our control' = C = what psychiatry does

Szasz's IDEAL (implied inverse): accept our actual responsibility
= 'we didn't fertilise the soil' = our error, our responsibility
= B: 'our responsibility for them' ✓

[The farmer should have said]
'Our harvest failed because we didn't farm properly'
= accepting OWN RESPONSIBILITY = B ✓

Instead they said:
'The deity intervened'
= problems beyond our control = C
= what Szasz mocks ✗

[Why C is Szasz's negative example]
C: 'often beyond our control'
= this IS the primitive religion position
= this IS the psychiatric position
= Szasz spends the whole passage criticising this attitude
= choosing C = agreeing with what Szasz attacks

[The 'implies' question technique]
When asked what the author IMPLIES:
= find the argument they are making
= the implied ideal = the OPPOSITE of what they criticise
= criticises deflection → implies acceptance of responsibility (B)
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q100): 'Implies' question = find the author's implied ideal = opposite of what they criticise. Szasz criticises: 'problems are beyond our control' (C). Szasz implies: 'accept our responsibility for them' (B). C = exactly the position Szasz attacks = eliminated.

quiz.question:
[Unit 105 — Meta Pattern]

Q97: chose C (analysing) — repress/sanitise/garbage disposal = hiding, not analysing
Q98: chose C (rectification) — positive word / Szasz says psychiatry HIDES not fixes
Q99: chose A (perspective) — too positive; analogy = blame deflection = liability avoidance
Q100: chose C (beyond control) — this IS what Szasz attacks, not what he implies

All four share one underlying error. Which correctly identifies it?
options: All four are vocabulary errors — unfamiliar words like sanitise and liability

All four = failing to apply Szasz's core thesis consistently. Szasz's argument: psychiatry = blame deflection tool that discourages accepting liability. Fix: before answering any question, state Szasz's thesis: 'psychiatry hides social problems by labelling them as mental illness, helping society avoid responsibility'. Then test each choice: does it match HIDING and BLAME DEFLECTION (Szasz's description of psychiatry)? Or does it match ACKNOWLEDGING RESPONSIBILITY (Szasz's implied ideal)?
All four are targeting errors — wrong paragraph
All four are paraphrasing errors only
quiz.answer: ② All four = not applying Szasz's thesis consistently. Thesis: psychiatry = blame deflection. Fix: classify each choice as 'matches hiding/deflection' or 'matches responsibility acceptance' before answering.
quiz.explanation:
[Szasz's core thesis]
Psychiatry = hides social problems + deflects responsibility onto 'mental illness'
= the opposite of acknowledging real human causes

[Four-question classification]
Q97: 'analysing' = confronting/revealing = opposite of hiding ✗ → 'hiding' = matches thesis ✓
Q98: 'rectification' = positive solving = opposite of hiding ✗ → 'symptoms not causes' = matches thesis ✓
Q99: 'perspective' = neutral/positive framing = opposite of blame deflection ✗ → 'discourages liability' = matches thesis ✓
Q100: 'beyond our control' = deflects responsibility = matches PSYCHIATRY not Szasz's ideal ✗ → 'our responsibility' = matches Szasz's implied opposite ✓

[The classification tool]
Before each question, ask:
'Does this choice describe HIDING/DEFLECTING (= psychiatry's function per Szasz)?'
'Or does this choice describe ACKNOWLEDGING/RESPONSIBILITY (= Szasz's implied ideal)?'

For Q97/98/99: correct answer = describes hiding/deflecting
For Q100 ('implies' question): correct answer = describes responsibility (the inverse)

[The four-word signature of Szasz]
REPRESS / SANITISE / GARBAGE DISPOSAL / PLACATE
= all mean: hide, remove, deflect
= any paraphrase of these = the 'hiding' cluster
quiz.action: Meta Rule (Unit 105): Szasz thesis = psychiatry hides social problems + deflects liability. Classify every choice: hiding/deflection (correct for descriptive questions) vs responsibility/acknowledgment (correct for 'implies what he values' questions). Four signature words: repress, sanitise, garbage disposal, placate = all mean 'hiding'.

quiz.question: [Q21 — Paraphrase: Buddhist attitude toward animals stems from the conviction that ______] Passage (lines 2–4): '...non-violence toward animals symbolises the reverence that one ought to have for all being, and at the FOUNDATION of this reverence is the PERVASIVE REALITY of the BUDDHA-NATURE (inner lightsomeness or intelligibility).' Q: The Buddhist attitude stems from the conviction that ______. Your pick — D: animals, being more helpless than humans, need protecting Correct — B: all of creation shares in the same spirit Why does 'pervasive reality of the Buddha-nature' = same spirit in all, not helplessness?
options: D is correct because Buddhist non-violence protects animals from human aggression

'Pervasive reality of the Buddha-nature' = Buddha-nature exists IN ALL BEINGS pervasively (everywhere). This is the FOUNDATION of reverence = the reason for non-violence is that all creatures share this universal spiritual reality (Buddha-nature = the same spirit). D: helplessness = a very different reason (animals need protection because they are weak) = not what the passage says. The reason is universal shared spiritual nature, not animal weakness.
B is wrong because the passage focuses on animals, not all of creation
D is correct because Buddhist ethics prioritises protecting the vulnerable
quiz.answer: ② 'Pervasive reality of Buddha-nature' = this spiritual reality exists in ALL beings = all creation shares the same spirit (B) / D = helplessness = wrong reason (passage says shared nature, not weakness)
quiz.explanation:
[Paraphrase chain → B]
'pervasive reality of the Buddha-nature'
= pervasive = present throughout ALL beings
= Buddha-nature = a shared universal spiritual quality
= FOUNDATION of reverence = why we respect animals
= because all creatures share this same spiritual reality

= 'all of creation shares in the SAME SPIRIT' = B ✓

[Why D (helplessness) fails]
helpless = weak, needing protection from external help
= this would make animal reverence = charity/protection
= but the passage says reverence is based on SHARED SPIRITUAL REALITY
= not on animal weakness

[The key concept: non-violence from shared nature]
If all beings share Buddha-nature:
= harming an animal = harming something that shares your own spiritual essence
= reverence comes from recognition of shared identity, not pity for the weak
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q21): 'Pervasive reality of Buddha-nature' = this spirit exists in ALL beings = shared universal nature = B. D (helplessness) = wrong cause: the passage says reverence is from shared spiritual nature, not animal weakness.

quiz.question: [Q22 — Targeting: Buddhist attitude ASSUMES that animals ______] Passage (lines 8–11): '...praise the SPONTANEITY of trees and animals, in contrast to the PAINFUL REFLECTIVITY of human beings. Whereas a pine tree or a fish just IS WHAT IT IS, without fuss and labour, a human being has to become human through education, experience, meditation...' Key contrast: Animals = spontaneous / no reflection / just ARE Humans = painfully reflective / must labour to become themselves Q: The Buddhist attitude assumes that animals ______. Your pick — B: are happy with their situation Correct — C: do not reason and reflect Why does 'painful reflectivity' specifically confirm C and rule out B?
options: B is correct because animals are described as spontaneous which implies contentment

'Painful reflectivity' = animals LACK reflectivity. 'A pine tree or fish just IS what it is' = they exist without the reasoning/reflection that humans use. C: 'do not reason and reflect' = direct paraphrase of 'without fuss, just is what it is'. B: 'happy with their situation' = emotional state = not what the passage describes. The passage focuses on COGNITIVE CAPACITY (reasoning/reflection), not emotional state (happiness).
C is wrong because the passage doesn't say animals lack intelligence
B is correct because 'spontaneous' implies a state of natural contentment
quiz.answer: ② 'Painful reflectivity' = humans have it, animals don't = animals do NOT reason/reflect (C) / B = happiness = wrong category (cognitive not emotional)
quiz.explanation:
[The contrast structure]
Animals: SPONTANEITY = 'just IS what it is, without fuss'
= no effort of reflection or reasoning
= simply exist as they are

Humans: PAINFUL REFLECTIVITY = must think, meditate, educate themselves
= the pain comes from over-thinking and self-consciousness

[Paraphrase chain → C]
animals don't have 'painful reflectivity'
= they don't reason and reflect (C) ✓

[Why B (happy with situation) fails]
B = an emotional state (happiness, contentment)
= the passage describes a COGNITIVE STATE (reasoning/reflection)
= the passage says animals are SPONTANEOUS, not that they are HAPPY
= spontaneous ≠ happy
= a pine tree is spontaneous — we don't say it is 'happy with its situation'
= B imports an emotional reading into a cognitive description ✗
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q22): 'Spontaneous' = not reflective (cognitive) NOT happy (emotional). 'Painful reflectivity' = humans reason, animals don't. C = do not reason and reflect = direct paraphrase. B = happiness = wrong category. Cognitive capacity ≠ emotional state.

quiz.question: [Q24 — Vocab: 'holistic' (line 13) is closest in meaning to ______] Context (lines 11–13): 'Whereas a pine tree or fish just is what it is, without fuss and labour, a human being has to BECOME human through education, experience, meditation... Japan always loved the image of HOLISTIC, spontaneous perfection.' = holistic perfection = the kind of perfection a pine tree HAS (not achieved in parts) = opposed to: piece-by-piece human self-improvement Q: 'Holistic' is closest in meaning to ______. A: total / B: sacred / C: hollow / D: impulsive Your pick — B: sacred Correct — A: total How does the pine tree context reveal 'total' over 'sacred'?
options: B is correct because Buddhist reverence makes the natural world sacred

The pine tree is 'holistic' in that it IS perfect as a WHOLE, with no missing parts — its perfection is complete and total, not built up in stages. 'Total' = the whole complete thing, nothing lacking. 'Sacred' = religiously holy = the passage discusses spiritual themes but holistic specifically means completeness/wholeness in this context. A: total = complete/whole = contrasts with humans who must work towards a partial, incomplete state.
A is wrong because 'total' doesn't capture the spiritual dimension of the passage
B is correct because the Japanese reverence for nature gives it a sacred quality
quiz.answer: ② Pine tree = perfect AS A WHOLE without parts needing completion = TOTAL (complete, whole) / sacred = religious holiness = different concept
quiz.explanation:
[Holistic decoded in context]
'holistic perfection' = contrasted with human becoming

Human: must become human through education, experience, meditation
= perfection assembled in PARTS over time
= never quite whole at any given moment

Pine tree: 'just IS what it is'
= its perfection is COMPLETE AS A WHOLE
= nothing missing, nothing to add
= TOTAL / WHOLE / COMPLETE perfection ✓

[Paraphrase chain → A]
holistic = of the whole, treating something as a complete unified system
= total = the whole thing, complete
= A ✓

[Why B (sacred) fails]
sacred = holy, religiously revered
= the passage is about Buddhist themes but 'holistic' is not about religious holiness
= it is about structural completeness vs partial assembly
= sacred adds a religious connotation that 'holistic' doesn't have here

[Holistic definition]
holistic = relating to the WHOLE rather than its parts
= total = the whole
= the pine tree's perfection = whole/total = A ✓
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q24): 'Holistic perfection' = perfection of the whole, not assembled in parts. Contrast: humans build themselves piece by piece / pine tree IS complete as a whole. Total = the whole = A. Sacred = religious = different concept. Context: completeness vs partial assembly.

quiz.question:
[Unit 104 — Meta Pattern]

Q21: chose D (helpless animals) — reverence from shared Buddha-nature, not animal weakness
Q22: chose B (happy with situation) — spontaneous = cognitive (no reflection), not emotional (happy)
Q24: chose B (sacred) — holistic = total/complete-as-whole, not religiously holy

All three errors share the same structural mistake. Which correctly identifies it?
options: All three are vocabulary errors — unfamiliar Buddhist terminology

All three = importing an EXTRA CONNOTATION not in the passage. Q21: 'helpless' = added emotional connotation of weakness (passage = shared spiritual nature). Q22: 'happy' = added emotional state (passage = cognitive state: no reflection). Q24: 'sacred' = added religious holiness (passage = structural completeness: whole vs parts). Fix: for each paraphrase, strip away additional connotations. Match only what the passage EXPLICITLY describes.
All three are targeting errors — wrong stanza analysed
All three are Too Extreme errors
quiz.answer: ② All three = added extra connotations: D adds helplessness / B adds happiness / B adds religious holiness. Fix: strip added connotations, match only what passage says.
quiz.explanation:
[The structural mistake: connotation addition]
Q21: passage says 'shared Buddha-nature' (spiritual unity)
D adds: helplessness/weakness = NOT in passage
Correct = all share same spirit (no added connotation) = B

Q22: passage says 'spontaneous, without fuss, just IS what it is' (cognitive)
B adds: happiness/contentment (emotional) = NOT in passage
Correct = no reasoning/reflection (stays cognitive) = C

Q24: passage says 'holistic perfection' in contrast to piecemeal human becoming
B adds: religious holiness (sacred) = NOT what holistic means here
Correct = total/complete (structural) = A

[The passage's actual contrast structure]
This passage is built on ONE central contrast:

NATURE (animals, pine trees, fish):
= spontaneous / holistic / no fuss / Buddha-nature present / no reflection

HUMAN BEINGS:
= painful reflectivity / piecemeal becoming / fuss and labour / must work to achieve

All questions in Unit 104 test whether you correctly read this contrast
= Q21: reverence = shared nature (not weakness)
= Q22: spontaneous = no reflection (not happy)
= Q24: holistic = whole/complete (not sacred)
quiz.action: Meta Rule (Unit 104): The passage's core structure = nature (spontaneous/whole/Buddha-nature) vs humans (reflective/piecemeal/labour). For each answer, strip any added emotional (happiness), weakness (helpless), or religious (sacred) connotations not in the passage. Match only what the contrast structure says.
